subject: Physics
q: If the mass of the bob of a pendulum is increased by a factor of \[3\], the period of the pendulum's motion will _____.
options: be increased by a factor of \[2\]
be decreased by a factor of \[2\]
be decreased by a factor of \[4\]
skill: Explain which forces act on a displaced pendulum and solve word problems of simple pendulums
feedback:
We have the following formula for the time period of a simple pendulum:

T = 2 pi sqrt{ l/g } \]

Where 'T' is the time period of the pendulum, 'l' is the length of pendulum and 'g' is the gravitational acceleration. Hence, time period of a pendulum is independent of the mass of its bob as we can see from the equation (there is no mass required in the calculation of the time period).

So, if the mass of the bob of a pendulum is increased by any factor, the period of the pendulum's motion will remain the same.

subject: Physics
q: If the length of a simple pendulum is doubled, what will be the change in its time period?
skill: Explain which forces act on a displaced pendulum and solve word problems of simple pendulums
feedback:
As the time period 'T' of a simple pendulum is: T \[ = 2 \pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} \]

When length is doubled, the new length is l \[= 2\]l

Now, T \[= 2 \pi \sqrt {\frac{2l}{g}} \]

or T \[ = 2 \pi \times \sqrt {2} \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} = \sqrt {2} \times ( 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}} ) = \sqrt{2}\] T as T \[ = 2 \pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} \]

Hence, T \[= \sqrt{2}\] T \[= 1.14\] T

Therefore, if the length of a simple pendulum is doubled, then its time period increases by a factor of \[1.41\].

subject: Physics
q: What happens to waves when they move from the deep end of a ripple tank to the shallow end?
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback: When water waves in a ripple tank travel from the deep end to the shallow end or from the shallow end to the deep end, their wavelengths change. This change in wavelength results in a change in direction of the waves. Therefore, when waves move from one medium to another, their direction changes. This change in direction is known as refraction.

subject: Physics
q: Given the following image of a ripple tank. Which of the following wave properties will be observed here?
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback: When waves pass through a small slit, they spread out in all directions. This makes the shape of the waves almost semi-circular. This spreading of waves when passing through a small slit is called diffraction.

subject: Physics
q: A student uses a ripple tank to create and study waves. At one end of the ripple tank, the water is shallow, while on the other end it is deep. Which of the following phenomena will the student observe in such a ripple tank?
options: The waves will bend as they move from the deep end to the shallow end.
The waves will get slower as they move from the deep end to the shallow end.
The waves will diffract as they move from the deep end to the shallow end.
The waves will bounce back as they move from the deep end to the shallow end.
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
The speed of a wave in water depends on the depth of water. Water waves get slower as they move from deep water to shallow water and vice versa. The reason for this is that the wavelength \[\lambda\] of the waves is longer in deep water and shorter in shallow water. The frequency of the waves is equal to the frequency of the vibrations of the paddle and so it is the same at both ends.
So, the frequency is the same but speed is faster in the deeper end as compared to the shallow end.



This can be verified by the formula for velocity of waves: \[ v=f \lambda\].
If the frequency is the same then the speed only depends on the wavelengths (\[\lambda\]). If the wavelength is shorter, the speed will be slower. If the wavelength is longer the speed will be faster.

subject: Physics
q: In a sound wave, the particles of the medium move back and forth, _____ to the direction of the wave. In a violet light wave, the particles of the medium move up and down, _____ to the direction of the wave.
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
Sound waves are longitudinal waves, whereas violet light waves are transverse waves.
In longitudinal waves, the particles of a medium move back and forth, parallel to the direction of the wave.
In transverse waves, the particles of a medium move up and down, perpendicular to the direction of the wave.

subject: Physics
q: Longitudinal waves consist of _____. And transverse waves consist of _____.
options: compressions and rarefactions; crests and troughs
crests and troughs; compressions and rarefactions
compressions and rarefactions; compressions and rarefactions
crests and troughs; crests and troughs
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
A compression and a rarefaction combine to form a longitudinal wave.
A sound wave is a type of longitudinal wave and is also made up of a compression and a rarefaction.

subject: Physics
q: Sara ties one end of a rope to a tree. She holds the other end of the rope and moves it up and down. What type of waves are produced?
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
As Sara moves the rope up and down, the wave travels away from her hand and towards the tree.
The direction of the movement of the particles is up and down but the direction of the wave is perpendicular to that movement. Therefore, the waves produced are transverse waves.

Longitudinal waves are also called compressional waves and sound waves are a type of longitudinal waves.

subject: Physics
q: Waves in which particles of a medium move parallel to the direction of a wave are called
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
Waves in which particles of a medium move in a direction parallel to the direction of a wave are called longitudinal waves.
X-rays are a type of electromagnetic wave. Electromagnetic waves are all transverse waves.

subject: Physics
q: What type of wave is shown in the figure below?
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback: The image shows longitudinal waves, also called compressional waves in a spring. The wave travels in a back and forth direction showing compressions where the spring is squashed together and rarefactions where the spring is stretched.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true about transverse waves?

I.   They consist of crests and troughs.
II.  Transverse waves can travel in a vacuum.
III. Light waves are a type of transverse wave.
IV. Particles of light waves move in the same direction as the wave.
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
Transverse waves consist of crests and troughs. Transverse waves do not need a material medium to travel from one place to another and light waves are a type of transverse wave.
Therefore, statements I, II, and III are true.
However, particles of transverse waves move in a direction perpendicular to the direction of the waves.

subject: Physics
q: Transverse waves are
options: waves made up of compressions and rarefactions.
waves where particles of a medium vibrate at a right angle to the direction the wave travels in.
waves where particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction the wave travels in.
waves where particles of the medium vibrate at an acute angle to the direction the wave travels in.
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
Waves in which particles of the medium vibrate at a right angle to the direction of wave are called transverse waves.
Compressions and rarefactions are part of longitudinal waves, which vibrate parallel to the direction of the wave.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following devices can be used to produce transverse and longitudinal waves\[?\]
skill: Identify transverse and longitudinal waves in mechanical media and describe properties of waves with a ripple tank
feedback:
Longitudinal and transverse waves can be produced with the help of a helical spring.

A ripple tank is used to produce water waves.

A tuning fork is used to produce sound waves.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following actions create electromagnetic waves?
skill: Describe wave motion with examples, and distinguish between mechanical and electromagnetic waves
feedback:
Lighting a matchstick creates light and heat. The light travels as a light wave and the heat travels as infra-red waves. Both of these waves are electromagnetic waves.

In all the other options, these actions are creating mechanical waves.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following waves are electromagnetic waves?
skill: Describe wave motion with examples, and distinguish between mechanical and electromagnetic waves
feedback:
X-rays are electromagnetic waves. They have the ability to go through the soft tissue in humans and animals but reflect after hitting our bones. This is why they are used to take images of the skeletons inside our bodies. These images are called X-rays.

Water and string waves are mechanical waves as they require a medium to travel through.

Heat waves are not exactly waves as we know them in physics. Heat waves only mean a duration or a period of intense heat like the ones we get in the summer. A misconception is that heat waves are electromagnetic waves. The waves that carry heat in the form of radiation are actually mostly infra-red waves or ultraviolet waves. In physics, they are not known as heat waves.

subject: Physics
q: Which letter represents the wave's trough?
skill: Define the terms (v), (f), (λ), (t), amplitude, crest, trough, wave cycle, compression, rarefaction
feedback:
The lowest point on a wave is its trough. D is the lowest point on this wave therefore, point D represents a trough.
Point E represents the highest point on a wave called a crest.

subject: Physics
q: The speed of a wave is measured in _____ and the frequency is measured in _____.
skill: Define the terms (v), (f), (λ), (t), amplitude, crest, trough, wave cycle, compression, rarefaction
feedback:
Speed is the distance travelled by wave in a unit time. Speed is measured in metres per second (m/s).
Frequency is the number of waves passing through a point in one second. Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz).
m/s2 is the unit for acceleration, not speed.

subject: Physics
q: The maximum displacement of a vibrating body is called _____.
skill: Define the terms (v), (f), (λ), (t), amplitude, crest, trough, wave cycle, compression, rarefaction
feedback:
The maximum displacement of a vibrating body is called amplitude.

The number of vibrations of a body in one second is called frequency.

A complete round trip of a body about its mean position is called one vibration.

subject: Physics
q: In the case of a vibratory motion, time period is the reciprocal of _____.
skill: Define the terms (v), (f), (λ), (t), amplitude, crest, trough, wave cycle, compression, rarefaction
feedback:
The time taken by a vibrating body to complete one vibration is called time period (\[T\] ).
It is the reciprocal of the frequency i.e. \[t=\frac{1}{f}\]

subject: Physics
q: In a vacuum, all electromagnetic waves have the same _____.
skill: Define the terms (v), (f), (λ), (t), amplitude, crest, trough, wave cycle, compression, rarefaction
feedback: All electromagnetic waves travel through vacuum at the same speed which is about \[3.0\times10^8\] meters per second. This is the speed at which light travels.

subject: Physics
q: What is the wavelength of a wave which has a velocity of \[25\] m/s and a frequency of \[10\] Hz?
skill: Derive equation v=f λ, and solve word problems by applying the relation f = 1/t and v= f λ
feedback:
To calculate the wavelength of a wave given its velocity and frequency, we will use the following equation that relates velocity, wavelength and frequency.

\begin{align} v &= f \lambda \\ \frac{v}{f} &= \lambda \\ \\ \lambda &= \frac{25}{10} \\ \\ &= 2.5 \mbox{ m} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: A water wave has a wavelength of \[0.5\] m and a frequency of \[2\] Hz. If it starts at one end of a \[50\] metres long swimming pool, how long will it take before it reaches the other end?
skill: Derive equation v=f λ, and solve word problems by applying the relation f = 1/t and v= f λ
feedback:
To find the time taken by the wave to travel \[50\] metres (which is the length of the pool), we need to know the velocity of the wave.

\begin{align} v &= f \lambda \\ &= (2)(0.5) \\ &= 1 \mbox{ m/s} \end{align}

To calculate the time taken, we need to rearrange the velocity equation.

\begin{align} v &= \frac{d}{t} \\ \\ t &= \frac{d}{v} \\ \\ &= \frac{50}{1} &= 50 \mbox{ s} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: Frequency is defined as the
options: number of waves passing through a point in 10 seconds.
type of waves passing through a point in one second.
type of waves passing through a point in 10 seconds.
number of waves passing through a point in one second.
skill: Derive equation v=f λ, and solve word problems by applying the relation f = 1/t and v= f λ
feedback:
The frequency of a wave is the number of complete waves passing a fixed point in one second. The formula for frequency is:

\[f = \frac{1}{t}\]

subject: Physics
q: The relation between v, \[f\], and \[\lambda\] of a wave is_____.
skill: Derive equation v=f λ, and solve word problems by applying the relation f = 1/t and v= f λ
feedback:
The relation between v, \[f\], and \[\lambda\] of a wave is v \[=\] \[f\] \[\lambda\]. This equation is true for both longitudinal and transverse waves.

We derive this equation from the formula: velocity \[= \frac {\mbox {distance} } { \mbox{time} } \]
v \[ = \frac{\mbox{d}} {\mbox{t}} \]

If t \[ =\] time period, then the distance(d) covered by the wave will be equal to one wavelength (\[\lambda\]) , hence we can write:
v \[ = \frac{\lambda} {\mbox{T}} \]

Time period (T) is the reciprocal of the frequency (f), i.e., T \[= \frac{1}{\mbox{f}}\]
Therefore, v \[=\] f \[\lambda\].

subject: Physics
q: The relation between \[\nu\], \[f\], and \[\lambda\] of a wave is
skill: Derive equation v=f λ, and solve word problems by applying the relation f = 1/t and v= f λ
feedback:
The relation between \[ u\], \[f\], and \[\lambda\] of a wave is \[ u\] \[=\] \[f\] \[\lambda\]. This equation is true for both longitudinal and transverse waves.

We derive this equation from the formula: \[velocity = rac{distance}{time}\]
\[v = rac{d}{t}\]

If \[t =\] Time period, then the distance (\[d\] ) covered by the wave will be equal to one wavelength \[\lambda\], hence we can write:

\[v = rac{\lambda}{T}\]
Time period (\[T\] ) is the reciprocal of the frequency (\[f\] ), i.e., \[T = rac{1}{f}\]
Therefore, \[ u\] \[=\] \[f\] \[\lambda\].

subject: Physics
q: Two astronauts try talking on the Moon but can't hear each other. They return to their spacecraft and now they can hear each other clearly. Why?
options: The moon does not allow the transfer of any type of energy in the form of waves.
Sound waves cannot travel in space or vacuum.
The air inside the spacecraft is a better conductor of sound waves than the air on the Moon.
skill: Describe wave motion with examples, and distinguish between mechanical and electromagnetic waves
feedback:
Sound waves are mechanical waves that need a material medium (metal, water or air) to travel and transfer energy. Mechanical waves cannot travel through a vacuum and the atmosphere of the Moon is a vacuum (no air or atmosphere). Astronauts cannot hear each other on the Moon because there is no medium to transfer sound waves. On the surface of the moon, they use their radio communication to talk to each other. Radio waves are electromagnetic waves and do not require a medium to travel.

However, their spacecraft is filled with air and oxygen and sound waves can travel inside the spacecraft through vibrations in the air.

subject: Physics
q: A wave is a _____.
options: disturbance that transfers only sound energy from one place to another
disturbance that transfers power from one place to another
disturbance that transfers energy from one place to another
disturbance that transfers only light energy from one place to another
skill: Describe wave motion with examples, and distinguish between mechanical and electromagnetic waves
feedback: A wave is a disturbance that transfers energy from one place to another. Mechanical waves are disturbances in matter (particles) that transfer the energy forward. An example is water waves (water particles move up and down to carry the energy). Electromagnetic waves are disturbances in electric and magnetic fields. That is why they do not need matter or medium to travel. They can travel across vacuums as well.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements is true about waves?
options: Sound and light waves can travel through a vacuum.
Sound waves need a medium to travel in, whereas light waves can travel through a vacuum.
Light waves need a medium to travel, whereas sound waves can travel through a vacuum.
Both sound waves and light waves need a medium to travel through.
skill: Describe wave motion with examples, and distinguish between mechanical and electromagnetic waves
feedback: Sound waves are longitudinal mechanical waves and they always need a medium (solid, liquid, gas) to travel through. On the other hand, light waves are electromagnetic waves and can travel through mediums as well as vacuum.

subject: Physics
q: Sound travels in waves, as shown in the image below. What type of wave is this?
skill: Explain the production of sound waves and their longitudinal nature
feedback: The image shows a longitudinal wave. Longitudinal waves are also called compressional waves. The wave travels in a back and forth direction showing compressions where the particles are squashed together and rarefactions where the particles are spaced out. This is how sound travels through a medium (solid, liquid, or gas).

subject: Physics
q: The speed of sound in iron at \[25 \ ^{\circ}\]C is _____.
skill: Explain the production of sound waves and their longitudinal nature
feedback:
Sound waves can travel through any medium containing particles that can vibrate. However, the nature of the medium will affect the speed of the sound waves.
Speed of sound in iron at \[25 \ ^{\circ}\]C is \[\bf 5,950\] ms\[\bf ^{-1}\].

subject: Physics
q: Astronauts in space need to communicate with each other by radio links because _____.
options: sound waves travel very slowly in space
sound waves travel very fast in space
sound waves cannot travel in space
sound waves have low frequency in space
skill: Explain the production of sound waves and their longitudinal nature
feedback: Sound waves travel by the vibration of particles of matter. Since space is a vacuum and does not contain any matter, sound waves cannot travel through it. But radio waves are electromagnetic waves that can travel through vacuum, therefore astronauts in space use radio links to communicate with each other.

subject: Physics
q: The speed of sound in solids is _____ than the speed of sound in gases.
skill: Explain the production of sound waves and their longitudinal nature
feedback: The speed of sound in solids is about fifteen times greater than the speed of sound in gases, while the speed of sound in a liquid is five times more than it is in a gas.

subject: Physics
q: At \[1\] atm pressure and \[21 \ ^{\circ}\]C (room temperature), the speed of sound in air is _____.
skill: Explain the production of sound waves and their longitudinal nature
feedback: At \[1\] atm pressure and \[21 \ ^{\circ}\]C (room temperature), the speed of sound in air is \[\bf 343\] ms\[\bf ^{-1}\].

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT true regarding sounds?
options: Sound is produced by a vibrating body.
The pitch of a sound depends on its amplitude.
Sound travels in the form of waves.
Sound is a form of energy.
skill: Define the terms pitch, loudness and quality of sound and describe the effects of change in amplitude and frequency
feedback: The pitch of a sound depends on its frequency and not on its amplitude. The higher the frequency, the shriller a sound may be.

subject: Physics
q: A sound with a higher frequency will have a greater
skill: Define the terms pitch, loudness and quality of sound and describe the effects of change in amplitude and frequency
feedback: The pitch of a sound depends upon its frequency. A greater frequency will result in a higher pitch sound. The loudness of a sound depends upon its amplitude. Infrasound is sound that is lower in frequency than 20 Hz.

subject: Physics
q: The _____ of a sound tells us how loud or soft a sound is.
skill: Define the terms pitch, loudness and quality of sound and describe the effects of change in amplitude and frequency
feedback: The loudness of a sound refers to how soft or loud a sound is. The pitch of a sound tells us how shrill or deep it is. The frequency of a sound is the number of complete waves passing a fixed point in 1 second.

subject: Physics
q: The _____ of a sound tells us how shrill or grave/deep it is.
skill: Define the terms pitch, loudness and quality of sound and describe the effects of change in amplitude and frequency
feedback:
A shrill sound can be distinguished from a grave or heavy sound by a characteristic of sound known as pitch.

The frequency of a sound is the number of complete waves passing a fixed point in 1 second.
The loudness of a sound basically tells us how loud or soft a sound is.

subject: Physics
q: By what factor is the speed of sound greater in water than in air?
skill: Define the terms pitch, loudness and quality of sound and describe the effects of change in amplitude and frequency
feedback:
The speed of sound waves can be affected by the medium through which they pass. The speed of sound in solids and liquids is faster than in air.
The speed of sound in liquids is \[\bf 5\] times greater than in air.

subject: Physics
q: Sound travels fastest in _____
skill: Describe how reflection of sound produces echo and how it is used to calculate speed of sound
feedback: Sound travels fastest in solids as molecules are more tightly packed together than in liquids or gases. Sound travels slowest in gases because they are the least dense. Sound does not travel in a vacuum because there are no molecules to vibrate.

subject: Physics
q:
Sounds can travel through _____.

I. solids
II. liquids
III. gases
IV. a vacuum
skill: Describe how reflection of sound produces echo and how it is used to calculate speed of sound
feedback: Sound waves are produced when a body vibrates. Therefore, sound requires a material, such as a solid, liquid, or gas to go through. Sound waves spread out from the source in all directions through a material medium from particle to particle.

subject: Physics
q:
How far away should the reflecting medium be from us for us to clearly hear any echo of the sounds that we make?

Speed of sound \[= 340\] ms\[^{-1}\]
skill: Describe how reflection of sound produces echo and how it is used to calculate speed of sound
feedback:
To hear a clear and distinct echo, the delay between the first sound and its echo should be more than \[0.1\] second otherwise our brain does not register the echo. So, to create a delay between the first sound and its echo, the reflecting medium needs to be far enough for the sound to take at least \[0.1\] second to get there and reflect back. Let's use the speed (\[=\] distance divided by time) to solve this problem:

Speed of sound \[= 340\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Required time \[= 0.1\] s
To calculate distance:

\begin{align} \mbox{speed} &= \frac{\mbox{distance}}{\mbox{time}} \\ \\ \mbox{distance} &= \mbox{speed} \times \mbox{time} \\ &= 340 \times 0.1 \\ &= 34 \mbox{ m} \end{align}

A very important thing to consider here is that \[34\] m is the total distance that the sound needs to travel. This means that it should be \[\frac{34}{2} = 17\] m to the reflecting medium and then \[17\] m back. So, the reflecting medium should be at least \[\bf17\] m away.

subject: Physics
q: When we hear a clear echo, the time difference between the first sound and the echoing sound is at least _____.
skill: Describe how reflection of sound produces echo and how it is used to calculate speed of sound
feedback: The sensation of any sound that we hear stays in our brains for \[\bf0.1\] second. What this means is that when we hear a sound, our brain continues hearing it for \[0.1\] second. Therefore, to hear a clear and distinct echo, the delay between the first sound and its echo should be more than \[0.1\] second otherwise our brain does not register the echo.

subject: Physics
q: Standing near a hill you make a loud noise and within a second you hear the same noise again. What causes this?
options: A nearby mobile phone records your sound and plays it within a second.
Somebody on the hill makes the same noise back at you.
The sound echoes and comes back to you.
When standing near a hill, our brains automatically create two sounds within a second.
skill: Describe how reflection of sound produces echo and how it is used to calculate speed of sound
feedback: When sound is incident on (comes into contact with) the surface of a different medium, some of it reflects back into the first medium. This is called an echo or reflection of sound. In this situation, the first medium through which your sound is travelling is air, and the mountain is the second medium, which reflects the sound back into the first medium.

subject: Physics
q:
An object produces a sound of \[52{,}000\] Hz. By using the table below, find out which animal can hear this sound?

animals frequency range (Hz)
horse 50 - 33,000
elephant 16 - 12,000
dog 15 - 50,000
cat 50 - 65,000
skill: Describe audible frequency range and the uses of ultrasound
feedback: The table shows the range of audible frequencies of a few animals. The frequency of the sound of the object is \[52{,}000\] hertz. A horse, elephant, and dog can hear ultrasonic sounds of \[33{,}000\] Hz, \[12{,}000\] Hz, and \[50{,}000\] Hz respectively. This indicates that these animals will not be able to hear this sound. Notice that the cat can hear sounds up to \[65{,}000\] hertz. Hence, the cat will be able to hear this sound.

subject: Physics
q: Sound waves with frequencies above \[20{,}000\] Hz are called ______
skill: Describe audible frequency range and the uses of ultrasound
feedback: Sound waves with frequencies above \[20{,}000\] Hz are inaudible to the human ear. They are called ultrasound. As humans cannot hear ultrasound, you may assume that it is useless to them, but that is not the case. Ultrasound has a lot of useful applications, especially in the field of healthcare and medicine, where it is used to detect objects and measure distances.

subject: Physics
q: The audible frequency range for an average human being is _____.
skill: Describe audible frequency range and the uses of ultrasound
feedback: The audible frequency range for an average human being is 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. It may decrease with age. Any sounds above or below this frequency range cannot be heard by humans. Animals, on the other hand, have different audible frequency ranges and can listen to sounds which humans cannot.

subject: Physics
q: The audible frequency range for human beings is from \[20\] Hz to _____.
skill: Describe audible frequency range and the uses of ultrasound
feedback: The audible frequency range for human beings is from \[20\] Hz to \[20{,}000\] Hz. This means that the human ear cannot register sounds with frequency less than \[20\] Hz or more than \[20{,}000\] Hz.

subject: Physics
q: For a normal person, the audible frequency range for sound waves lies between _____.
skill: Describe audible frequency range and the uses of ultrasound
feedback: The audible frequency range for normal human beings is from \[\bf 20\] Hz to \[\bf 20\] kHz. This means that the human ear cannot register sounds with a frequency less than \[20\] Hz or more than \[20\] kHz.

subject: Physics
q: Noise is _____.
options: a sound that is produced by non-living things
sudden and irregular bursts of sound
skill: Explain noise pollution, and the importance of acoustic protection
feedback: Noise consists of sudden and irregular bursts of sound. Noise can also be low volume sounds such as a fan running but not all low volume sounds are noise. Echoed sounds are used in music and are not necessarily noise. Humans talking can also be noise, for example, a loud classroom in which all the students are talking is noisy even though it is just sounds of humans talking.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following sounds is likely to be pleasant and entertaining?
options: the sound of a smoke detector
the cries of a hungry baby
the sound of a flute charming a snake
the siren of a police vehicle
skill: Explain noise pollution, and the importance of acoustic protection
feedback:
Generally, music, radio, and television provide us with entertainment.
We find music enjoyable and pleasurable. Therefore, the sounds of musical instruments such as the flute, violin, and guitar are pleasant and entertaining. The correct response is the sound of a flute charming a snake.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following sounds are noises?
skill: Explain noise pollution, and the importance of acoustic protection
feedback:
In science, pleasant sounds are musical, as the changes in the frequency and amplitude of their waves are regular and gradual. The sounds of a flute, guitar, and violin are pleasant if played well.

Irregular and abrupt changes in the frequency and amplitude of sound waves produce noise. So, the sound of traffic would be considered noise since it is caused by the loud and unpleasant honking of cars.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is a pleasant sound?
options: the shrill sound of a drilling machine
skill: Explain noise pollution, and the importance of acoustic protection
feedback:
In science, pleasant sounds are musical, as the changes in the frequency and amplitude of their waves are regular and gradual. Since the sound of a flute is musical, it is, therefore, a pleasant sound.

Irregular and abrupt changes in the frequency and amplitude of sound waves produce noise. The loud horn of a train, the shrill sound of a drilling machine, and traffic noise have irregular changes in their frequencies and amplitudes.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements describing noise is incorrect?
options: Noise is produced by regular vibrations.
Noise has a jarring and unpleasant effect.
Noise is produced by sudden vibrations.
skill: Explain noise pollution, and the importance of acoustic protection
feedback: Noise is a sound which has a jarring and unpleasant effect on our ears. Irregular and sudden vibrations usually produce noise.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following options is \[0\] bel?
skill: Define and describe intensity level of sound, and solve word problems
feedback: Bel is a unit named after Alexander Graham Bell, the scientist who invented telephones. \[0\] bel is the lowest intensity of sound that can be heard by humans. In Wm \[^{-2}\] that intensity is \[\bf 10^{-12}\] Wm\[\bf ^{-2}\].

subject: Physics
q: The loudness of a sound is related to the intensity of that sound. The more energy per second per area that a sound has (known as its intensity), the louder it is. Loudness of a sound is directly proportional to the _____ of intensity.
skill: Define and describe intensity level of sound, and solve word problems
feedback:
Loudness (\[L\]) of a sound is directly proportional to the logarithm of its intensity. Mathematically, it looks like this:

\begin{align} L &\propto \log I \\ L &= K \log I \end{align}
Here, \[K\] is a constant of proportionality.

subject: Physics
q: What is the SI unit of intensity of sound?
skill: Define and describe intensity level of sound, and solve word problems
feedback:
The SI unit of intensity of sound is Wm\[^{-2}\]. This means watts per metre square area. We know that the watt is a unit for power. Therefore, the unit of sound intensity tells us the power per unit area created by that sound. So, the intensity of a sound tells us how much power that sound can create when crossing through a given area.

Remember: power is simply energy per unit time. So, intensity of sound tells us the energy per second that passes through a metre square of area.

subject: Physics
q: The intensity of the rustling of leaves is _____.
skill: Define and describe intensity level of sound, and solve word problems
feedback:
Sound energy passing per second through a unit area held perpendicular to the direction of propagation of sound waves is called intensity of sound.

The intensity of the rustling of leaves is \[\bf 10\] dB.

subject: Physics
q: One bel is equal to _____.
skill: Define and describe intensity level of sound, and solve word problems
feedback:
The 'bel' and 'decibels' (dB) are units of measurement of sound intensity.

\[1\] bel = \[\bf 10\] dB

subject: Physics
q: The angle that the incident ray makes with the normal line is called the _____.
skill: State the law of reflection of light and describe the key terms
feedback: The angle that the incident ray makes with the normal, when it falls onto the surface of an object, is called the angle of incidence. It is denoted by \[i\].

subject: Physics
q: The ray of light that falls on a surface is called the _____ ray, while the ray that bounces back is called the _____ ray.
skill: State the law of reflection of light and describe the key terms
feedback: The ray of light that falls on a surface is called the incident ray, and the ray of light that bounces back from a surface is called the reflected ray.

subject: Physics
q: We can see ourselves in a mirror because of the _____ of light.
skill: State the law of reflection of light and describe the key terms
feedback:
We can see ourselves in a mirror due to the reflection of light.
Light falls onto a surface and bounces back, this is called the reflection of light.

subject: Physics
q: The focal length of a spherical mirror is related to the radius of its curvature by _____.
skill: State the law of reflection of light and describe the key terms
feedback: The focal length is the distance between the optical centre and the principal focus and it is related to the radius of curvature by \[f =\frac{R}{2}\]. It means that as the radius of curvature is reduced, the focal length of the reflecting surface decreases.

subject: Physics
q: In a concave mirror, the _____ is/are reflecting.
options: sides of the mirror, the inner and the outer curved surfaces
skill: State the law of reflection of light and describe the key terms
feedback:
* In a concave mirror the inner curved surface is reflecting.
* In a convex mirror the outer curved surface is reflecting.

subject: Physics
q: If a ray of light in a glass is incident on an air surface at an angle greater than the critical angle, the ray will _____.
skill: State the law of reflection of light and describe the key terms
feedback:
In general, reflection or refraction happens when light passes from one medium to another.
If a ray of light in a glass is incident on an air surface at an angle greater than the critical angle, the ray will reflect only.

subject: Physics
q: If an object is located beyond the focal point of a concave mirror, its image will be _____
topic: Image location by spherical mirror equation
skill: Solve word problems related to the image location by spherical mirrors using mirror formula
feedback: When an object is placed beyond the focal point of a concave mirror, its image is both inverted and real. This is because the light rays from the top of the object fall onto the top of the mirror and are reflected downwards. The light rays from the bottom of the object fall onto the bottom of the mirror and reflect upwards. As a result, we see an inverted image.

subject: Physics
q: The mirror you usually see in a bathroom is classified as a _____ mirror.
topic: Image location by spherical mirror equation
skill: Solve word problems related to the image location by spherical mirrors using mirror formula
feedback: The mirrors generally used in bathrooms show an image of the same size as the object in front of them. Forming an image of the same size and shape as the object in front of it, is a characteristic of a plane mirror. Therefore, a bathroom mirror is a plane mirror.

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows image formation by a plane mirror. If the size of the actual flame is 2 cm,what is the size of the flame in the image?
options: half the size of the actual flame
double the size of the actual flame
equal to the size of the actual flame
three times the size of the actual flame
topic: Image location by spherical mirror equation
skill: Solve word problems related to the image location by spherical mirrors using mirror formula
feedback: The image formed by a plane mirror is a duplicate of the object placed in front of it. Therefore, the size of the flame in the image has the same size as the actual flame. It will be equal to 2 cm.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements are true for an image formed by a plane mirror?
options: The distance of the image appears to be the same behind the mirror as the distance of the object in front of it.
It appears smaller than the object placed in front of the mirror.
It is virtual and larger than the object itself.
topic: Image location by spherical mirror equation
skill: Solve word problems related to the image location by spherical mirrors using mirror formula
feedback: The virtual image formed by a plane mirror appears to be behind the mirror. The distance between this image and the mirror appears to be the same as the distance between the mirror and the object in front of it.

subject: Physics
q: An object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave mirror. The image produced by the mirror is located _____.
options: out beyond the centre of the curvature
at the centre of the curvature
between the centre of the curvature and focal point
topic: Image location by spherical mirror equation
skill: Solve word problems related to the image location by spherical mirrors using mirror formula
feedback: When an object is placed at the centre of the curvature of a concave mirror, the image produced by the mirror is located at the same place as the object, at the centre of curvature. All light rays coming from the centre of curvature hit the surface perpendicular to it and therefore reflect back towards the same location.

subject: Physics
q: A stick immersed in water looks bent due to the _____ of light.
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
When a stick is dipped partly in water, it appears to be broken at the intersection of water and air. This is due to the property of refraction of light.

The speed of light is different in different mediums. There are rare and dense mediums. A rarer medium is lighter, such as air. Water and glass are examples of dense mediums. The speed of light is slow in dense and fast in rare mediums. Therefore, when light passes from a dense into a rare medium, it bends due to the difference in speed.

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a material is called
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a material is called the refractive index of the material.
When light enters from one medium to another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called refraction.
Reflection is the bouncing back of light when it hits an object. The splitting of a ray of light into seven colours after passing through a prism is called the dispersion of light.

subject: Physics
q: When light enters one medium from another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
When light enters one medium from another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called refraction.
Reflection is light bouncing back when it hits an object. The splitting of a ray of light into seven colours after passing through a prism is called the dispersion of light. Rotation refers to the rotation of an object about an axis or centre.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following quantities remains unchanged during refraction of light\[?\]
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback: In refraction, the speed of light changes due to a change in the wavelength. But the frequency of light does not change. The direction of light also changes during refraction.

subject: Physics
q: We get a critical angle when
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback: \[\angle r = 90^\circ\] when the angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle. In other words, when \[\angle i =\angle \text C\] the angle of refraction is \[90^\circ\] and total internal reflection starts.

subject: Physics
q:
An incident ray A is making an angle of incidence at \[42^\circ\] in a glass. Which ray of light shows the refracted ray?

The critical angle of glass with respect to air is \[42 ^{\circ}\]
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback: As \[42^\circ\] is the critical angle of glass the angle of refraction should be \[90^\circ\]. In this case, the refracted ray does not enter the air. Instead, it moves along the boundary of the glass. Hence, the correct refracted ray is C.

subject: Physics
q: For the critical angle, at what degree should the angle of refraction be?
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback: When light rays pass from a dense medium like glass or water to a rare medium like air, they bend away from the normal. The angle of refraction is greater than the angle of incidence. If the angle of incidence is gradually increased, a point will come when maximum refraction occurs, and the angle of refraction becomes \[90^\circ\]. Such an angle is called the critical angle.

subject: Physics
q: The angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction is \[90^\circ\] is called the _____
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback: The angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction is \[90^\circ\] is called the critical angle. This is important to know because if the angle of incidence is bigger than the critical angle then no refraction occurs, and all of the light is reflected. This is called total internal reflection.

subject: Physics
q: What is the refractive index of water\[?\]
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback:
The refractive index (\[n\]) of a medium is the ratio of the speed of light (\[c\]) in air to the speed (\[v\]) of light in the medium:

Refractive index \[ = \] Speed of light in air / Speed of light in medium
or \[n = \frac{c}{v}\]

The refractive index of water is \[1.33\].

subject: Physics
q: The index of refraction depends on _____.
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback:
The index of refraction of a medium is the ratio of the speed of light in air to the speed of light in the medium.

Refractive Index \[ = \frac {\mbox{Speed of light in air}} {\mbox{Speed of light in medium}} \]

So, the index of refraction depends on the speed of light.

subject: Physics
q: If the refractive index of ice is \[1.31\], find the critical angle.
skill: Describe the relation between critical angle and refractive index and solve word problems
feedback:
Refractive index \[ n =1.31\]

Critical angle 'C' is given as:

\[Sin\] C \[ =\frac{1}{n} \] or C \[ = Sin^{-1} \frac{1}{n} = Sin^{-1} \frac{1}{1.31} = Sin^{-1} (0.76) = 49.7^{\circ} \]

subject: Physics
q: A/an _____ is used to carry information between two places in the form of light.
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback: An optical fibre cable is made up of thin strands of glass or plastic that carry information between two places in the form of light. They use total internal reflection to carry the light within the cable; the light hits the cladding of an optical fibre cable and reflects back into the core.

subject: Physics
q: Total internal reflection occurs when
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback: When \[\angle i\] is equal to \[\ \angle \text C\], the refracted ray moves along the boundary of a dense medium. When the angle of incidence becomes greater than the critical angle, all the rays reflect back in the dense material. Hence, \[\angle i > \angle \text C\] is the condition required for total internal reflection.

subject: Physics
q: Which figure shows total internal reflection?
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback:
When a ray of light passes from a dense medium to a rare medium, it refracts and bends away from normal. The angle of refraction is greater than the angle of incidence. In case the angle of incidence becomes greater than the critical angle, the refracted ray does not enter a rarer medium. Rather, it is reflected in the same medium. This is called total internal reflection, which has been shown by the figure:


Note that the image that shows the refracted ray going exactly at \[90^{\circ}\] from the normal is not an example of total internal reflection. Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is greater than the critical angle.

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the correct statement/statements regarding total internal reflection.

i.    Light rays must travel from a dense to a rare medium.
ii.   Light rays travel from one rare to another rare medium.
iii. The angle of incidence of all the rays will be greater than the angle of their refraction.
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback:
There are two necessary conditions for total internal reflection to happen.

1. The ray of light must be traveling from a dense medium to a rare medium.
2. The angle of incidence must be greater than the critical angle.

So, statements I and III are correct.

subject: Physics
q: At which point should the fisherman aim to catch a fish? Note that there is only one fish and the remaining are all perceived images of the fish.
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback: C and D are not correct as the incident ray, when entering the air from water, refracts away from normal. The incident ray from B travels without bending in either medium. It should bend because it meets the water at an angle. The real position of the fish is at A. Light refracts away from the normal when it enters air from water. Since light travels in a straight line, the fisherman may be able to see the image at B. The fisherman should aim at point A so that he does not miss the fish.

subject: Physics
q: The critical angle for a beam of light passing from water into air is \[48.8\] degrees. This means that all light rays with an angle of incidence greater than this angle will be _____.
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback: When the angle of incidence of light passing from water into air becomes larger than the critical angle of water i.e. \[48.8^{\circ}\], the entire light is reflected back into the denser medium. This is known as total internal reflection of light.

subject: Physics
q: An endoscope that is used to examine bladders is called a _____.
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback:
An endoscope is a medical instrument (based on the phenomena of total internal reflection) used to explore and examine the interior organs of the body.

Cystoscope is an endoscope used to examine bladder.
Gastroscope is an endoscope used to examine stomach.
Bronchoscope is an endoscope used to examine throat.

subject: Physics
q: The inner part of fibre optics is called the _____.
skill: Define total internal reflection and describe its use in light propagation
feedback:
Fibre optics is a flexible fibre that is used in the telecommunications field. The concept of total internal reflection is mostly used in fibre optics.

The inner part of the fibre optics is called the core.

The outer shell of the fibre optics is called cladding.

subject: Physics
q: Calculate the speed of light in a diamond if the refractive index is \[2.4\].
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
The refractive index is given by the formula:

\[\text {Refractive index} = \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in a diamond}}\]

Since the speed of light in air is almost equal to the speed of light in the vacuum, we replace the values of the speed of light in a vacuum with the speed of light in air.

By putting values into the formula, we get:

\[\eta = \frac{\text{speed of light in the air}}{\text{speed of light in the diamond}}\]

\[2.4 = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}{\text{speed of light in the medium }}\]


\[2.4 \times {\text{speed of light in a diamond }} = {3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}\]


\[{\text{speed of light in a diamond }} = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}{\text{2.4 }}\]


\[{\text{speed of light in a diamond }} = 1.25 \times 10^{8}\text{ms}^{-1} \]

subject: Physics
q:
Find the refractive index of glass if the speed of light through glass is \[2 \times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}\].
(Note that the speed of light in air is \[3 \times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}\].)
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
A refractive index is found by the formula:

\[\text {Refractive index} = \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in the medium}}\]

Since the speed of light in air is almost equal to the speed of light in the vacuum, we replace the values of the speed of light in a vacuum with the speed of light in air.

By putting values into this formula, we get:

\[\eta = \frac{\text{speed of light in a vacuum}}{\text{speed of light in the medium}}\]

\[\eta = \frac{3\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}{2\times 10^{8} \text{ms}^{-1}}\].


\[\eta = \frac{3}{2}\]

\[\eta = 1.5\]

subject: Physics
q: What happens to the light spectrum at point Y?
options: It passes through without changing direction.
It bends away from the normal.
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
As the spectrum of light leaves the prism, it enters a less dense medium and therefore bends away from the normal.
Reflection is incorrect because the prism is not a mirror. The spectrum of light does not pass through the prism without changing direction because it always exits at an angle to the surface of the prism.

subject: Physics
q: What happens to white light at point X?
options: It bends away from the normal.
It passes through without changing direction.
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
The dispersion of white light as it passes through the prism is an example of refraction.
As white light enters the prism, it enters a denser medium and therefore bends towards the normal.
Reflection is incorrect because the prism is not a mirror. The white light does not pass through the prism without changing direction, because it is always at an angle to the surface of the prism.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following statements are TRUE regarding the law of refraction?

I.    The incident ray, the refracted ray, and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
II.   The angle of incidence and the angle of refraction are always equal.
III. The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to its speed in another medium is always constant.
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
There are two laws of refraction.

1. The incident ray, the refracted ray, and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
2. The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to its speed in another medium is always constant.

When a ray of light passes from a rare medium to a dense medium, \[\angle r< \angle i\]  and vice versa. So, statement II is not correct.

Hence, statements I and III are correct.

skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism

it changes speed as well as direction.
it changes its speed, but not the direction.
it does not change direction or speed.

skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism

skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism

subject: Physics

skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism

The speed of light is slow in dense and fast in rare mediums. Therefore, when light passes from a dense into a rare medium, it bends due to the difference in speed.

q: The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a material is called

feedback:
The ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a material is called the refractive index of the material.

q: When light enters one medium from another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback:
When light enters one medium from another, it bends slightly from its original path. This bending of light is called refraction.
Reflection is light bouncing back when it hits an object. The splitting of a ray of light into seven colours after passing through a prism is called the dispersion of light. Rotation refers to the rotation of an object about an axis or centre.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following quantities remains unchanged during refraction of light\[?\]
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback: In refraction, the speed of light changes due to a change in the wavelength. But the frequency of light does not change. The direction of light also changes during refraction.

subject: Physics
q: Rainbows are formed due to _____.
skill: State the law of refraction and demonstrate the path of a light ray through a parallel sided transparent material and a glass prism
feedback: Dispersion in drops of water in the air separates the colours of sunlight into the different colours of the rainbow .

subject: Physics
q: A telescope uses a combination of   _____ lenses.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A simple astronomical lens or telescope uses two convex lenses. One is referred to as the objective lens and the other as the eye piece.

subject: Physics
q: Which ray becomes parallel to the principle axis after passing through the lens?
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The principle axis of a lens is an imaginary line drawn through the centre of the lens and is perpendicular to the surface of the lens. The light ray C is parallel to the principle axis after refracting through the lens.

subject: Physics
q: Identify the ray/rays passing through the optical centre.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The centre of the lens is known as the optical centre. It is denoted by O. Ray A is the only one that goes through the optical centre.

subject: Physics
q: A ray pointed at the focal point on the other side of a concave lens becomes _______ after passing through the lens.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A ray passing through the focal point on the other side of a concave lens becomes parallel to the principle axis after passing through the lens.

subject: Physics
q: If the image is located at twice the focal length of a convex lens (at 2F), where will the image form?
options: at F on the opposite side
at F on the same side
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: If the object is at 2F, the image of the object will form at 2F on the other side of the convex lens.  According to the rules of refraction, when a ray of light passes through a convex lens, it refracts and will always pass through the focal point on the other side. The image will form at the point where the rays intersect on the other side of the convex lens.

subject: Physics
q: If the object is beyond twice the focal length of a convex lens (beyond 2F), the image on the other side of the lens will be formed _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: If the object is beyond twice the focal length, the image formed by a convex lens will always lie between F and 2F regardless of the distance at which the object actually lies.

subject: Physics
q: Real images are _____ while virtual images are _____ when they pass through a lens.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A real image occurs where rays converge and are inverted, whereas a virtual image occurs where rays only appear to converge and appear upright. Real images can be produced by concave mirrors and convex lenses, but only if the object is placed further away from the mirror/lens than the focal point.

subject: Physics
q: The image made by a concave lens is _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: Concave lenses make virtual images. The rays always diverge through a concave lens after refracting, so the  rays do not actually meet. Therefore, a real image is not formed.

subject: Physics
q: The image made by a convex lens is _____ when the object is beyond the focal point.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The Image made by a convex lens is inverted when the object is far away from focal point. For example, when the object is placed beyond F, the image formed is real, inverted, and larger in size than the object itself.

subject: Physics
q: Rays passing through the optical centre of a convex or concave lens ______.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: Rays passing through the optical centre of a lens do not refract. A light passing through the principle axis passes through without changing its direction.

subject: Physics
q: What is focal length?
options: the distance between two focal points
the distance between the centre of a lens and its focal point
the length of the principal axis
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The distance between the centre of a lens and its focal point is known as the focal length.

subject: Physics
q: A lens that causes rays to diverge is called a _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The ray diagram shows how parallel rays diverge as they pass through a simple concave lens. Convex lenses cause rays to converge. A plane mirror would result in the reflection of these rays, whereas a flat lens would allow the rays to pass through and remain parallel.

subject: Physics
q: The point denoted as F in the diagram is _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The focal point is defined as the point where all parallel rays meet after refracting through a curved surface.

subject: Physics
q: The point marked O in the diagram stands for _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The centre of the lens is known as the optical centre. It is denoted by O.

subject: Physics
q: The parallel rays converge as they pass through a _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The ray diagram shows how parallel rays converge as they pass through a simple convex lens. Concave lenses cause rays to diverge, whereas a plane mirror results in the reflection of these rays. A flat lens allows the rays to pass through and remain parallel.

subject: Physics
q: What is another term for a convex lens?
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A convex lens is also called a converging lens because all the light rays passing through it converge at a single point. A convex lens is thicker at the centre and thinner at the edges.

subject: Physics
q: The distance between the optical centre and the principal focus of a lens is called _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The distance from the centre of the lens to the principal focus, or focal point, is called the focal length. Parallel rays that enter a converging lens come together at the focal point before spreading out.

subject: Physics
q: A lens that diverges light rays is called a _____.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A concave lens, which is also called a diverging lens, diverges the light rays that pass through it. When light rays from an object pass through a concave lens, the refracted rays diverge and do not meet at a single point. Hence, a real image is not formed.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following descriptions describes the image that will form given the following ray diagram?
options: at 2F, real, inverted, same size as the object
beyond 2F, real, inverted, larger than the object
behind the object, virtual, erect, larger than the object
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback:
The following ray diagram shows the type of image that will be formed and its exact location.
The image is formed behind the object. Moreover, it is virtual, erect, and larger than the object (as you can see). Please ignore the labels 'focal', 'parallel', and 'axial'. These are only used to help create ray diagrams with accurate image locations.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following descriptions describes the image that will form given the following ray diagram?
options: at 2F, real, inverted, same size as the object
beyond 2F, real, inverted, larger than the object
between F and 2F, real, inverted, smaller than the object
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: For convex lenses, when the object is at F, no image of the object can be formed. This is because the refracted rays are parallel and never merge to form an image.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following descriptions describes the image that will form given the following ray diagram?
options: at \[2F\], real, inverted, same size as the object
beyond \[2F\], real, inverted, larger than the object
between \[F\] and \[2F\], real, inverted, smaller than the object
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback:
For convex lenses, when the object is between \[F\] and \[2F\], the image of the object is:
  1. beyond \[2F\]
  2. real, inverted
  3. larger than the object

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following descriptions describes the image that will form given the following ray diagram?
options: at 2F, real, inverted, same size as the object
beyond 2F, real, inverted, larger than the object
between F and 2F, real, inverted, smaller than the object
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback:
The following ray diagram shows what type of image will form and its location:


The image formed is between F and 2F. Moreover, it is real and inverted, and is smaller than the object (as you can see).

subject: Physics
q: In the given ray diagram, what do I and II represent?
options: I: principal focus, II: focal point
I: focal point, II: optical centre
I: optical centre, II: principal focus
I: principal focus, II: optical centre
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: The centre of the lens that lies on the principal axis is called the optical centre. Therefore, point I is the optical centre of the lens. The ray that runs parallel to the principal axis, intersects the principal axis at the principal focus after it is refracted by the lens. We can see in the image that the topmost ray is parallel to the principal axis that goes through the centre of the lens. The point where that ray intersects with the principal axis (which is point II in this case) is called the principal focus.

subject: Physics
q: A/An _____ image is an image where rays appear to converge.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback:
A virtual image is an image where refracted rays appear to converge. A real image, on the other hand, is an image where refracted rays actuallyconverge.

In optics, a virtual image is an image formed when the outgoing rays from a point always diverge. Because the rays never really converge, a virtual image cannot be projected onto a screen. The following diagrams clarify what makes real and virtual images.

subject: Physics
q: An object placed in front of a concave lens will produce an image that is _____, _____, and  _____ than the object.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A concave lens is thicker at the edges and thinner in the middle. When an object is placed in front of a concave lens, the image produced will be virtual, upright, and smaller than the object. This image and the object will always be located on the same side of the lens.

subject: Physics
q: Identify the type of lens that will cause parallel rays of light to converge at a single point.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback: A convex lens is thicker at the centre and thinner at the edges. When parallel rays of light pass through a convex lens, the refracted rays converge at a single point. This is why convex lenses are called converging lenses. This point of convergence is known as the focal point or the principal focus.

subject: Physics
q: Identify point X in the given diagram.
skill: Demonstrate that light is refracted through lenses and draw images by placing objects in front of convex and concave lenses
feedback:
The principal focus of a convex lens is the point in space where parallel light rays meet after passing through the lens. A perfect lens would send all light rays through one principal focus, which would result in the clearest image. The focal point is also known as the focal point.

The focal length of a convex lens is the distance between the centre of the lens and the focal point.

subject: Physics
q:
The formula used to determine the magnifying power of a simple microscope is as follows:

\[M =\] _____

Fill in the blank.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a simple and compound microscope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback:
The magnifying power of a simple microscope can be calculated using the formula,

\[ M = \frac{ \ \ \theta'}{\theta}\]

And the formula,

\[M = 1 +\frac{d}{f}\], where \[d\] is the near point of the viewer's eye and \[f\] is the focal length of the lens. The magnifying power of a compound microscope is given by:

\[M = \frac{L}{f_o} (1 +\frac{d}{f_o})\]

subject: Physics
q: The following ray diagram shows which one of the following devices?
skill: Describe the working and uses of a simple and compound microscope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: The ray-diagram shows a convex lens being used to produce a magnified virtual image. A convex lens used in such a way is called a magnifying glass. Magnifying glasses are known as simple microscopes.

subject: Physics
q: The ability to distinguish or differentiate between two closely placed objects or point sources is called _____.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a simple and compound microscope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: Resolving power refers to the ability of an instrument (e.g. a microscope or telescope) to distinguish between two closely placed objects. For example, a microscope with a high resolving power is required to look at a molecule of CO\[_2\] and distinguish between atoms of oxygen and carbon.

subject: Physics
q: What does the word micro mean?
skill: Describe the working and uses of a simple and compound microscope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: Micro means small. We use microscopes to observe small objects.

subject: Physics
q: A simple microscope is another name for _____.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a simple and compound microscope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: To produce a magnified image of a small object, a magnifying glass is used. A magnifying glass is also called a simple microscope. A simple microscope contains a convex lens which magnifies the image of an object that is placed between the lens and its focal length. You can test this when you use a magnifying glass by moving the object that you want to look at further and further away.

subject: Physics
q: Identify the type of telescope and the image it forms.
options: It is a reflecting telescope and forms a magnified real image.
It is a refracting telescope and forms a magnified virtual image.
It is a radio telescope and forms a magnified real image.
It is a non-optical telescope and forms a magnified virtual image.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a telescope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: A refracting telescope uses an objective lens and an eyepiece to gather more light than the human eye is able to collect on its own. The two convex lenses focus light and a brighter, clearer, magnified virtual image is formed.

subject: Physics
q: Observe the diagram and choose the correct type of telescope.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a telescope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: Refracting telescopes contain two convex lenses: an objective lens and an eyepiece lens. They are both fixed in a long tube. Light comes in from a distant object, enters the objective lens, and refracts to a point called the principal focus of the objective lens. The eyepiece lens receives the light from the objective lens and creates a virtual image. This virtual image appears closer to our eyes than the real object, which is why it looks bigger than the object.

subject: Physics
q: The _____ telescope uses a pair of convex lenses to focus light and make distant objects appear clearer and magnified.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a telescope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: A refracting telescope uses an objective lens and an eyepiece lens to gather more light than the human eye is able to collect on its own. The two convex lenses focus light to form a brighter, clearer, and magnified image.

subject: Physics
q: Which instrument helps us to see distant objects in the universe?
skill: Describe the working and uses of a telescope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: When an object is far away, less of its light will reach our eyes compared to a nearby object. The image takes up less space on our retina, making the image smaller. This makes details of the image harder to see. To make a distant object appear brighter and larger, we need a bigger eye to collect more light. A telescope is an amazing device as it functions like such an eye and allows us to see distant objects.

subject: Physics
q: The instrument used to see distant objects is called a _____.
skill: Describe the working and uses of a telescope, draw its ray diagram and mention its magnifying power
feedback: An optical instrument which is used to observe distant objects using lenses or mirrors is called a telescope.

subject: Physics
q: Nadia is short-sighted. She tries a pair of convex lenses to fix her problem, however, she still cannot see distant objects clearly. Why?
options: She needs a pair of concave lenses to fix her problem.
The convex lenses she tried are too weak.
The concave lenses she tried are too strong.
Images now form on her retina. She needs lenses which form images behind her retina.
skill: State the causes, draw ray diagrams, and describe the correction of short-sightedness and long-sightedness
feedback:
A short-sighted person can see objects that are nearby clearly. Objects at a distance are blurred. This problem can be corrected by wearing a pair of concave lenses with the appropriate focal length.

Convex lenses are used to correct long-sightedness, not short-sightedness.

subject: Physics
q: Sara is walking outside on a sunny day. She puts on sunglasses. What happens to her eyes?
options: The focal length of the lenses in her eyes increases.
The focal length of the lenses in her eyes decreases.
skill: State the causes, draw ray diagrams, and describe the correction of short-sightedness and long-sightedness
feedback:
The effect of putting on sunglasses is similar to walking into a dark room. In both cases, the pupils will expand, so more light can enter the eye.

The pupil of an eye expands when the radial muscles of the iris contract and the circular muscles relax.

subject: Physics
q: Which part of the eye refracts light?
skill: State the causes, draw ray diagrams, and describe the correction of short-sightedness and long-sightedness
feedback:
The cornea is a transparent layer on the outer surface of the eye. Both the cornea and the lens of an eye refract light.

The iris is a coloured circular structure that controls the size of the pupil. The pupil in turn controls the amount of light that enters the eye. Images in the eye form on the retina. The iris, pupil, and retina play no part in refracting light.

subject: Physics
q: Which type of image is produced by converging the lens of a human eye, if it views a distant object\[?\]
skill: State the causes, draw ray diagrams, and describe the correction of short-sightedness and long-sightedness
feedback:
When a human eye views a distant object, a real image is formed on the retina and the image is upside down (inverted). The image is also smaller than the real object.
So, the image produced by converging the lens of human eye is real, inverted, and diminished.

subject: Physics
q: The image formed on a camera is _____.
skill: State the causes, draw ray diagrams, and describe the correction of short-sightedness and long-sightedness
feedback: A simple camera has a convex lens in it and the image formed on it is real, inverted, and diminished.

subject: Physics
q: In the last orbit electrons are under less force of attraction of the _____.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: When electrons are in the last orbit, they are the farthest from the force of attraction from the nucleus of the atoms. The nucleus of the atoms hold protons which carry positive charge and therefore attract the negatively charged electrons.

subject: Physics
q: If a solid body loses electrons it becomes _____ charged.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: If a body loses electrons, it goes from being neutral to being positively charged.

subject: Physics
q: If a solid body gains electrons it becomes _____ charged.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: If a body gains electrons, it becomes negative charged as electrons carry the negative charge.

subject: Physics
q: Solid bodies are charged by _____.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: Solid bodies are charged when they loose or gain electrons. When they loose electrons they become positively charged. When they gain electrons, they become negatively charged.

subject: Physics
q: In normal conditions a solid body is _____.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: In normal conditions, all the solid bodies around us are neutral. They are provided charge when rubbed against another material etc.

subject: Physics
q: By rubbing a glass rod on silk a deficiency of _____  is produced on the glass rod.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
