feedback: When a glass rod is rubbed against silk, the glass rod becomes positively charged and the silk becomes negatively charged. This means that the glass rod passes some of its electrons to the silk which then becomes negatively charged. Can you tell which charge the glass rod has then?

subject: Physics
q: _____ charge is produced by rubbing plastic rod on cotton.
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: When a plastic rod is rubbed against cotton cloth, there is negative charge produced on the plastic rod. But there is also positive charged produced on the cotton cloth. When the cotton cloth gives the plastic rod some of its electrons to make it negatively charged, the cotton cloth itself becomes positively charged as it has lost electrons.

subject: Physics
q: Charge can be produced by
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: Charges can be produced even when two neutral substances are rubbed together. Examples of this include plastic combs rubbing against our hair, glass rods rubbed against silk, and also rubbing plastic on fur.

subject: Physics
q: Two charged spheres are separated by \[2\] mm. Which of the following would produce the greatest attractive force?
skill: Show the production of electric charges with experiments
feedback: \[+1q\] and \[-2q\] would produce the greatest attractive force as these are the only unlike charges. All the other charges are like charges (either both positive or both negative), so they would repel each other.

subject: Physics
q: If the leaves of an electroscope diverge when touched by an object, the object is _____.
skill: Describe electrostatic charging by induction and explain the construction and working principle of an electroscope
feedback: When a charged object touches the disk of an electroscope, it passes off its charge to the electroscope. This makes the leaves of the electroscope diverge.

subject: Physics
q: With the help of an electroscope, we can find if a body is:
skill: Describe electrostatic charging by induction and explain the construction and working principle of an electroscope
feedback:
We can use an electroscope to tell if a body is charged (positively or negatively). We can also use an electroscope to test if a body is a conductor or an insulator. Can you tell how?

If a body touches the brass disk of a charged electroscope, the leaves will come closer if the body is a conductor, and will stay the same if the body is an insulator.

subject: Physics
q: When a charged body is brought near a negatively charged electroscope, the gold leaves become more diverged. This means that the charged body has _____ charge.
skill: Describe electrostatic charging by induction and explain the construction and working principle of an electroscope
feedback: When the leaves of a charged electroscope diverge more than they already are when a charged body is brought near it, it means the electroscope and the charged body has the same charge. If the charged body and the electroscope have opposite charges, then the leaves will become less diverged and will move closer to each other.

subject: Physics
q: To make an electroscope positively charged, a _____ body is touched to the brass disk of the electroscope.
skill: Describe electrostatic charging by induction and explain the construction and working principle of an electroscope
feedback: When a positively charged body touches the brass disk of an electroscope, the electrons - or negative charges - are transferred to the positively charged body, which leaves the electroscope positively charged.

subject: Physics
q: If a negatively charged body is brought near an electroscope, its gold leaves will become _____.
skill: Describe electrostatic charging by induction and explain the construction and working principle of an electroscope
feedback: If a negatively charged body is brought near an electroscope, the brass disk at the top will become positively charged as the negative charges on the body will repel the negative charges of the neutral disk towards the gold leaves. This will make the brass disk positively charged and the gold leaves, at the end of the brass rod connecting the brass disk to the leaves, will become negatively charged.

subject: Physics
q: If a neutral body is brought near an electroscope its leaves will _____.
skill: Describe electrostatic charging by induction and explain the construction and working principle of an electroscope
feedback: If a body that is neutral (not negatively or positively charged) is brought near an electroscope, the gold leaves of the electroscope will not move.

subject: Physics
q: If \[4.8 \times 10^{-19}\] joules of energy is supplied to this charge \[q\] to move it from \[A\] to \[B\] through a potential difference of \[3\] volts, what is the charge?
options: neither and electron nor a proton
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
\begin{align} \triangle V&=\frac{\triangle \mbox{EPE}}{q}\\\\ \Rightarrow q&=\frac{\triangle \mbox{EPE}}{\triangle V}\\\\ &=\frac{4.8 \times 10^{-19}}{3}\\\\ &=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mbox{ C} \end{align}

Since energy is being supplied to this charge to move it in the direction of the electric field from \[A\] to \[B\], we can understand that it should be getting repelled by the negative plate. Since a negative charge would only be repelled by a negative plate, therefore \[q=-1.6 \times 10^{-19}\] coulomb, which is the charge of an electron.

If, instead, the same amount of energy was being supplied to this charge to move it from \[B\] to \[A\], through the same potential difference, what would the charge be?

subject: Physics
q: What is the electric potential energy of a charge of \[4 \space \mu\]C at a point in an electric field having a potential of \[0.4\] kV?
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback: \begin{align} \mbox{EPE}&=qV\\ &=(0.4 \times 10^3) \times (4 \times 10^{-6})\\ &=1.6 \times 10^{(3-6)}=1.6 \times 10^{-3}\\ \therefore \mbox{EPE}&=1.6 \mbox{ mJ} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: If \[12\] joules of work is done in bringing a charge of \[3\] coulombs to a certain point in an electric field, what is the electric potential at that point?
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback: \begin{align} V&=\frac{W}{q}\\\\ &=\frac{12}{3}\\\\ \therefore V&=4 \mbox{ V} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: For a charge of \[1\] coulomb, the potential difference between two points becomes _____ the energy supplied by/to the charge as it moves between the points.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
The electric potential difference between two points is simply the difference of electric potentials at those points in an electric field.
Since the electric potential at a point in an electric field is the electric potential energy per unit charge at that point, electric potential difference is the difference of electric potential energies per unit charge at two points in an electric field.

\begin{align} \triangle V&=\frac{\triangle \mbox{EPE}}{q}\\\\ \mbox{For } q&=1 \mbox{ C},\\\\ \triangle V&=\triangle \mbox{EPE} \end{align}

So, for a charge of \[1\] coulomb, the potential difference between two points becomes equal to the energy supplied by/to the charge as it moves between the points.

subject: Physics
q: If \[q\] is to move from \[B\] to \[A\], it will be going _____ the electric field, and an energy of _____ will need to be supplied _____ it.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
If \[q\] is to move from \[B\] to \[A\], it will be going against the electric field, and an energy of \[q(V_B-V_A)\] will need to be supplied to it.

Since the electric field is directed from \[A\] to \[B\], if \[q\] (being a positive charge) moves from \[B\] to \[A\], it will be going against the direction of the electric field.
Because like charges repel, some work will have to be done or energy will have to be applied to push \[q\] towards the positive plate. In other words, energy will be supplied to it.

To find how much energy will be supplied to the charge, we can utilise our knowledge about the electric potential difference between two points in an electric field.
Electric potential difference is simply the difference of electric potentials or the difference of electric potential energies per unit charge as it moves from one point to the other in an electric field.

\begin{align} \triangle V&=\frac{\triangle \mbox{EPE}}{q}\\\\ \Rightarrow \triangle \mbox{EPE}&=q\triangle V\\ \therefore \triangle \mbox{EPE}&=q(V_B-V_A) &(\because q \mbox{ moves from }B \mbox{ to }A) \end{align}

subject: Physics
q:
From the following, pick the CORRECT statements about electric potential difference.

I.    It is the difference of electric potentials at two points in an electric field.

II.   It is the difference of electric potentials per unit charge at two points in an electric field.

III. It is the difference of electric potential energies at two points in an electric field.

IV. It is the difference of electric potential energies per unit charge at two points in an electric field.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
Electric potential difference is simply the difference of electric potentials at two points in an electric field.

Since the electric potential at a point in an electric field is the electric potential energy per unit charge at that point, electric potential difference can also be defined as the difference of electric potential energies per unit charge at two points in an electric field. It is the work done in taking the charge from one point to the other. It is commonly called 'voltage'.

\begin{align} V_{AB}&=V_A-V_B\\\\ &=\frac{\mbox{EPE}_A}{q}-\frac{\mbox{EPE}_B}{q}\\\\ \Rightarrow V_{AB}&=\frac{\mbox{EPE}_A-\mbox{EPE}_B}{q}\\\\ \therefore \triangle V&=\frac{\triangle \mbox{EPE}}{q} \end{align}

So, statements I and IV are correct.

subject: Physics
q: Pick the WRONG statement.
options: If \[q\] is positive, energy will be supplied by it, as it moves in the direction of the field.
If \[q\] is negative, energy will be supplied to it, as it moves in the direction of the field.
If \[q\] is positive, energy will be supplied by it, as it moves against the direction of the field.
If \[q\] is negative, energy will be supplied by it, as it moves against the direction of the field.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
If \[q\] is negative, energy will be supplied to it, as it moves in the direction of the field from \[A\] to \[B\]. This is because like charges repel, and some work will have to be done or energy will have to be applied to push it towards the negative plate.
However, energy will be supplied by it, as it moves against the direction of the field from \[B\] to \[A\], because unlike charges attract, and it will be naturally drawn to the positive plate. While that happens, \[q\] will provide some energy.

If \[q\] is positive, energy will be supplied by it, as it moves in the direction of the field from \[A\] to \[B\]. This is because unlike charges attract, and so it will naturally be drawn to the negative plate. While that happens, \[q\] will provide energy as the negative plate pulls it. In other words, energy will be supplied by it.
However, energy will be supplied TO it, as it moves against the direction of the field from \[B\] to \[A\], because like charges repel, and some work will have to be done or energy will have to be applied to push it towards the positive plate.

Therefore, the statement saying that energy will be supplied BY a positive charge as it moves against the direction of the field, is WRONG. That process clearly USES UP energy, not provide it.

subject: Physics
q: What is TRUE about electric potential energy and electric potential?
options: Electric potential energy is a vector quantity, while electric potential is a scalar quantity.
Electric potential energy is a scalar quantity, while electric potential is a vector quantity.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
Electric potential "energy" is a scalar quantity.
Since electric potential is the 'electric potential energy per unit charge' at a point in an electric field, it is also scalar because it is a ratio of two scalar quantities: electric potential energy and charge.

subject: Physics
q:
Pick the CORRECT statements.

I.    As it moves from \[A\] to \[B\], energy will be supplied by \[q\].

II.   As it moves from \[A\] to \[B\], energy will be supplied to \[q\].

III. As it moves from \[B\] to \[A\], energy will be supplied by \[q\].

IV. As it moves from \[B\] to \[A\], energy will be supplied to \[q\].
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
Since \[q\] is negative charge, if it moves from \[A\] to \[B\], it will be moving in the direction of the electric field. Because like charges repel, some work will have to be done or energy will have to be applied to push \[q\] towards the negative plate. Therefore, we can say that energy will be supplied to it.
Hence, statement I is wrong, and statement II is CORRECT.

Energy will be supplied by the charge \[q\], when it moves against the direction of the electric field from \[B\] to \[A\]. Since \[q\] is a negative charge and unlike charges attract, it will naturally be drawn to the positive plate. While that happens, \[q\] will provide energy as the positive plate pulls it.
Therefore, statement III is CORRECT, and statement IV is wrong.

subject: Physics
q:
Pick the CORRECT statements.

I.    As it moves from \[A\] to \[B\], energy will be supplied by \[q\].

II.   As it moves from \[A\] to \[B\], energy will be supplied to \[q\].

III. As it moves from \[B\] to \[A\], energy will be supplied by \[q\].

IV. As it moves from \[B\] to \[A\], energy will be supplied to \[q\].
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
Since \[q\] is a positive charge, if it moves from \[A\] to \[B\], it will be moving in the direction of the electric field. It will naturally be drawn to the negative plate, because opposite charges attract. While that happens, \[q\] will provide energy as it moves towards the negative plate. In other words, energy will be supplied by it.
Therefore, statement I is CORRECT, and statement II is wrong.

Energy needs to be supplied to the charge \[q\], to make it move against the direction of the electric field from \[B\] to \[A\]. Since \[q\] is a positive charge and because like charges repel, some work will have to be done or energy will have to be applied to push it towards the positive plate.
Therefore, statement IV is CORRECT, and statement III is wrong.

subject: Physics
q: If the electric potential at a point in an electric field is known, the electric potential energy of a charge at that point is calculated by _____.
options: multiplying the electric potential with the charge
dividing the electric potential by the charge
dividing the charge by the electric potential
adding the electric potential and the charge
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
The electric potential at a point in an electric field is the electric potential energy per unit charge at that point.

\begin{align} V&=\frac{\mbox{EPE}}{q}\\\\ \Rightarrow \mbox{EPE}&=qV \end{align}

Therefore, if the electric potential at a point in an electric field is known, the electric potential energy of a charge at that point is calculated by multiplying the electric potential with the charge.

subject: Physics
q: Electric potential energy is measured in _____ and electric potential is measured in _____.
options: joules per coulomb; joules per coulomb
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
Electric potential energy is measured in joules because it is "energy", the SI unit of which is joules.
Electric potential is measured in joules per coulomb since it is the 'electric potential energy per unit charge' at a point in an electric field (where the SI unit of charge is coulomb).

subject: Physics
q: The SI unit of electric potential is _____.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
The electric potential at a point in an electric field is the electric potential energy of a unit charge placed at that point. It is the work done on a unit charge to bring it from infinity (where the electric potential energy is approximately zero) to that point.

\begin{align} V&=\frac{\mbox{EPE}}{q}=\frac{W}{q}\\\\ \mbox{Unit of } V&=\frac{\mbox{J}}{\mbox{C}}=\mbox{V} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q:
From the following, pick the CORRECT statement(s) about the electric potential at a point in an electric field.

I.    It is the electric potential energy at that point.

II.   It is the electric potential energy per unit charge at that point.

III. It is the electrostatic force per unit charge at that point.

IV. It is the work done on a unit charge to bring it from infinity to that point.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
The electric potential at a point in an electric field is not the electric potential energy but the electric potential energy per unit charge at that point.

\[V=\frac{\mbox{EPE}}{q}\]

Therefore, statement I is wrong and statement II is CORRECT.

The electrostatic force per unit charge at a point in an electric field is the electric field intensity at that point.
Therefore, statement III is wrong.

The electric potential at a point in an electric field is the work done on a unit charge to bring it from infinity to that point.

\[V=\frac{W}{q}\]

Therefore, statement IV is CORRECT.

subject: Physics
q: The electric potential at a point in an electric field is the _____ of a unit positive charge placed at that point.
skill: Describe the concept of electrostatic potential and potential difference
feedback:
The electric potential at a point in an electric field is the electric potential energy of a unit positive charge placed at that point.

It is the electric potential energy per unit charge or work done on a unit charge to bring it from infinity to that point.

Additional Explanation: The electrostatic force at infinity, with respect to a particular point in an electric field, would be approximately zero. We know this because electrostatic force varies inversely with the square of distance: as distance increases, the force decreases. (Coulomb's Law: \[F \propto \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\])

Therefore, work (being a function of force) or electric potential energy would also be approximately zero at infinity.

\[V=\frac{\mbox{EPE}}{q}=\frac{W}{q}\]

Electrostatic force per unit charge at a point in an electric field is the electric field intensity at that point.

Gravitational potential energy per unit mass at a point in a gravitational field is the gravitational potential of the mass at that point.
We can use gravitational potential energy as an analogy to understand electric potential. For instance, as an object in a gravitational field would move from a point of higher potential energy to a point of lower potential energy, in an electric field, a charge also tends to move from a point of higher electric potential energy to a point of lower electric potential energy.

subject: Physics
q:
Cloud-to-ground lightning can cause _____.

I.   property damage
II.  fires
III. electrocution
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback: Cloud-to-ground lightning, caused by the contact of negatively charged thunderclouds with positively charged grounded objects, is one of the most dangerous types of lightning. It carries millions of volts of electricity that can cause explosions and destroy property. It also happens to be extremely hot, going over \[50{,}000\] degrees Fahrenheit, resulting in drastic fires. It can also electrocute living beings, causing lifelong injuries or instant death.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following is not a danger of static electricity?

I.   electrostatic attraction between charges during car painting
II.  friction between the fuel and the pipe while putting fuel into aircraft
III. lightning during a thunderstorm
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback: Friction between the fuel and the pipe while putting fuel into aircraft can cause sparks and eventually explosions. This danger can be avoided by grounding the pipe. Lightning during thunderstorms, that occurs due to electrostatically induced charge from the charged thunderclouds, can cause massive fires. Electrostatic attraction between charges during car painting helps the paint reach even the hard-to-get places, giving the car a fine painted finish.

subject: Physics
q: _____ is NOT an application of electrostatics.
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback: Car painting, inkjet printing, and electrostatic air cleaning are all applications of electrostatics. House painting, however, is not, as it is carried out by regular painting methods using liquid paint and paintbrush.

subject: Physics
q: Which type of capacitors are used in tuning circuits of radios?
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback:
Capacitors can distinguish between high frequency signals and low frequency signals, which is required in many electronic circuits. Radio sets use variable (or tuning) capacitors in their circuits so they can be tuned to different frequencies. To switch to different frequencies, the capacitance is varied in these circuits. These tuning circuits are called resonant circuits, or sometimes filter circuits.
Variable capacitors usually have air as an insulator. Paper, electrolytic, and mica capacitors are all examples of fixed capacitors.

subject: Physics
q: Pick the CORRECT statement.
options: Capacitors block alternating current and allow direct current to pass through.
Capacitors block direct current and allow alternating current to pass through.
Capacitors do not allow direct current or alternating current to pass through.
Capacitors allow both direct current and alternating current to pass through.
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback:
Capacitors are charge-storing devices, consisting of two metallic plates separated by an insulator. When a capacitor is connected to a voltage source, the metallic plates start getting charged. Once the respective charges have accumulated on both the plates, and the capacitor is fully charged, it does not allow current to pass through it.
When a capacitor is connected to a DC voltage source, direct current flows through it. Since direct current flows in only one direction, once the capacitor gets charged, it does not allow DC to pass through.
When a capacitor is connected to an AC voltage source, alternating current flows through it. Alternating current periodically reverses its direction. When it's flowing in one direction, the capacitor gets charged. As the current reverses its direction, the capacitor starts getting discharged, and the process keeps repeating periodically.
Therefore, capacitors allow alternating current to pass through because they can get charged and discharged in regular cycles. But since direct current is constant, once a capacitor gets charged, it has no room to let current pass. This goes to show that capacitors block direct current.

subject: Physics
q:
Capacitors are used in _____.

I.   fans
II.  air conditioners
III. washing machines
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback: Capacitors are used in a variety of electrical and electronic devices. In appliances like fans, air conditioners, and washing machines, capacitors are used to make their operation smooth and increase their lifespan. Direct application of power to an appliance can damage it. With a capacitor in the circuit, since it stores and releases charge slowly, the power provided to the appliance rises smoothly, protecting the appliance from a power surge and improving its performance.

subject: Physics
q: When you rub a plastic rod against your hair several times and put it near some bits of paper, the pieces of paper are attracted towards it. What does this observation indicate\[?\]
options: The rod and the paper are oppositely charged.
The rod acquires a positive charge.
The rod and the paper have the same charges.
The rod acquires a negative charge.
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback:
This observation indicates that pieces of paper and a plastic rod attract each other because opposite charges attract each other.

This statement suggests that the rod and the paper are oppositely charged.

subject: Physics
q: Two uncharged objects \[A\] and \[B\] are rubbed against each other. When object \[B\] is placed near a negatively charged object \[C\], the two objects repel each other. Which of these statements is about object \[A?\]
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback:
When two neutral objects are rubbed against each other, electrons get transferred from one object to the other. An object that loses electrons acquires a positive charge, while an object that gains electrons acquires a negative charge.

In this activity, after acquiring charges, object \[B\] is repelled by a negatively charged object \[C\]. Keeping in mind that like charges repel each other, we conclude that object \[B\] becomes negatively charged and object \[A\] becomes positively charged after transferring some of its electrons to \[B\].

subject: Physics
q: An object gains excess negative charge after being rubbed against another object, which is _____.
options: either neutral, negatively charged, or positively charged
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback:
When an object is rubbed against another neutral body, it steals electrons from the neutral body and gains excess negative charge.

For example, when we rub a rubber balloon against our hair, it steals electrons from our hair and gains excess negative charge.

subject: Physics
q: A positive electric charge _____.
skill: Describe the applications of capacitors and electrostatics and the dangers of static electricity
feedback: Like charges repel each other, while unlike charges attract each other. So, a positive charge always repels other positive charges.

subject: Physics
q: Two capacitors, in series, are connected to a battery of \[V\] volts. If \[C_1=3C_2\], \[V_1=\]_____.
skill: Derive and apply the formula for capacitance of a few capacitors connected in series and parallel
feedback:
When capacitors that are in series with each other are connected to a battery, the amount of charge developed between the plates of each capacitor is the same. The voltage that the battery provides, however, is divided amongst the capacitors and varies with the capacitance of each.

\begin{align} V_2&=\frac{Q}{C_2}\\\\ V_1&=\frac{Q}{C_1}\\\\ &=\frac{Q}{3C_2} &(\because C_1=3C_2)\\\\ &=\frac{C_2 V_2}{3C_2} &(\because Q=C_2 V_2)\\\\ \therefore V_1&=\frac{V_2}{3} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor is a device that stores _____.
skill: Derive and apply the formula for capacitance of a few capacitors connected in series and parallel
feedback: A capacitor is a device that stores an electric charge between two oppositely charged metallic plates separated by a dielectric.

subject: Physics
q: The SI unit of capacitance is _____.
skill: Derive and apply the formula for capacitance of a few capacitors connected in series and parallel
feedback:
* SI unit of capacitance is farad.

* SI unit of charge is coulomb.

* SI unit of electric current is ampere.

* SI unit of energy is joule.

subject: Physics
q: One picofarad (pF) is equal to _____ F.
skill: Derive and apply the formula for capacitance of a few capacitors connected in series and parallel
feedback:
The SI unit of capacitance is the farad (F). A farad is a very large unit, so we usually use a smaller unit such as the picofarad (pF).

\[1\] pico farad \[=1\times10^{-12}\] F
\[1\] nano farad \[=1\times10^{-9}\] F
\[1\] micro farad \[=1\times10^{-6}\] F

subject: Physics
q: Capacitance is defined as _____.
skill: Derive and apply the formula for capacitance of a few capacitors connected in series and parallel
feedback:
Capacitance is the ability of electrical conductors and insulators to store electric charge when a potential difference exists between the conductors. The symbol for capacitance is \[C\] and it is defined as a ratio of the electric charge \[Q\] (that is stored between the metallic plates) to the voltage \[V\] across the plates.

\[C = \frac{Q}{V}\]

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is the unit of charge?
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback: Electric charge is measured in coulombs. This unit is named after a French scientist, Charles Coulomb, who experimented on and modelled the attraction and repulsion of electric charges.

subject: Physics
q: Current (\[I\]) is equal to ____ .
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback:
According to its definition, current is the rate of flow of electric charge through any cross-sectional area. Whenever we talk about the rate of anything, we mean how much time it takes for that to happen. For example, speed is the rate at which distance is covered. So in its formula, we have distance divided by time. Current is the rate of flow of electric charge, so current is charge divided by time:

\[\mbox{Current} = \frac{\mbox{Charge}}{\mbox{Time}} = \frac{Q}{t}\]

subject: Physics
q: A battery converts chemical energy into _____ energy.
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback: Batteries convert chemical energy into electrical energy. This energy is then used in the circuits to which the batteries are connected.

subject: Physics
q: The flow of current in conductors is due to ____.
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback:
The flow of current just means the flow of charge. Electrons carry a negative charge so the flow of electrons means it is also a flow of charge and is therefore the flow of current. Protons are also positively charged but they never flow as they are bound in the nucleus of the atoms and are not free to move.

Ions are also particles that carry charge. Therefore, the flow of current in conductors can be due to ions. Batteries are an example of current flowing in conductors due to ions. This process also takes place inside any liquid that conducts electricity.

subject: Physics
q: The rate of flow of charge through any cross-sectional area is called _____.
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback:
When electric charges start moving in one direction due to an external electric field, we say that electric current is flowing. The definition of electric current is that it is the rate of flow of charge through any cross-sectional area. The formula for electric current according to its definition is:

\[\mbox{Current} = \frac{\mbox{Charge}}{\mbox{Time}}\]

subject: Physics
q: The ampere is a unit of _____.
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback: The SI unit for the measurement of electric current is ampere (A).

subject: Physics
q: An electric current in solid conductors is due to the flow of _____.
skill: Define electric current and differentiate between conventional and non-conventional current
feedback:
In conductors, the electric current is produced only due to the flow of free electrons, which carry negative charges.

Electric current can also be produced due to the flow of positive charges, like in an electrolyte.

subject: Physics
q: What is the potential difference across the \[3 \ \Omega\] resistor in the circuit shown below?
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: The potential difference supplied to all the resisters is \[6\] V. In parallel connections, the potential difference stays the same across all parallel components of a circuit.

subject: Physics
q: If the emf of each cell is \[1.5\] V, what is the voltage across the resistor \[R\] ?
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: If we count the number of cells we get \[4\]. You can confirm this by counting the longer lines or the shorter lines. 4 longer lines means there are \[4\] cells. Each cell provides an emf of \[1.5\] V. In total the voltage provided is \[1.5 \times 4 = 6\] V.

subject: Physics
q: What is emf or electromotive force?
options: It is the work done by an appliance to drive electrons around a circuit.
It is the work done by a source to drive a unit charge around a circuit.
It is the work done to drive a unit charge through a component.
It is the product of current and resistance.
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: Emf is the energy supplied by a battery to a unit positive charge when it flows through a closed circuit. We know that work done is also quantified as energy. So emf is the work done by a battery or power source to drive a unit charge in a circuit.

subject: Physics
q: The emf of a battery is \[10\] V. How much energy is supplied by the battery to \[2\] C of charge?
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback:
We will use the formula for emf of a battery to calculate the energy it supplies to the charge. The formula for emf is:

\begin{align} \mbox{emf} &= \frac{\mbox{Energy}}{\mbox{Charge}} \\ \\ \mbox{Energy} &= \mbox{emf} \times \mbox{Charge} \\ &= 10 \times 2 &= 20 \mbox{ J} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: What happens to the intensity or brightness of lamps connected in series as more and more lamps are added, connected across the batteries?
options: We cannot predict what will happen.
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: If the same batteries are connected, this means that potential difference across the circuit is the same. When we add more lamps in series, the potential difference across each lamp decreases. For instance, if the batteries provide \[10\] V of potential difference, one lamp will get the full \[10\] V of potential difference. If we connect a second similar lamp in series with the first one, the potential difference will be \[5\] V for each lamp. If we connect \[5\] lamps, they will each get \[2\] V of potential difference. This will reduce the brightness of the lamps as more and more are connected.

subject: Physics
q: The unit of potential difference is _____.
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: The potential difference between two points is measured in volts. The unit "volt" is named after an Italian physicist Alessandro Volta who developed the first practical battery. Because it is measured in volts, its potential difference is also called 'voltage'.

subject: Physics
q: The unit of emf which is equal to the volt in the SI system is _____.
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: In the SI system, the unit of emf is the volt (V) which is equal to JC\[^{-1}\]. J (joule) is the unit of energy and C (coulomb) is the unit of charge.

subject: Physics
q: The SI unit of emf is volt, which is equal to _____.
skill: Differentiate between potential difference and electromotive force
feedback: In SI system, the unit of emf is volt (V) which is equal to JC\[^{-1}\], where J (joule) is the unit of energy and C (coulomb) is the unit of charge.

subject: Physics
q: The unit of (\[\rho\]) in the formula \[R=\frac{\rho L}{A}\] is _____.
skill: Describe Ohm's law and its limitations and define and describe resistance
feedback:
\[\rho\] is a constant of proportionality and is known as the specific resistance of a material. The unit of \[\rho\] can be calculated by inputting the units of the other variables in the formula and solving for \[\rho\]:

\begin{align} R&=\frac{\rho L}{A} \\ \\ \Rightarrow \rho&=\frac{RA}{L} \\ \\ \therefore \mbox{Unit of } \rho &=\frac{\Omega \mbox{m}^2}{\mbox{m}} \\ \\ \Rightarrow \mbox{Unit of } \rho &=\Omega \mbox{m} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: The resistance in conductors is due to _____.
skill: Describe Ohm's law and its limitations and define and describe resistance
feedback: The resistance experienced in conductors of electricity is due to the collision of the moving electrons with the fixed atoms of the conducting material.

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following is ohmic in nature\[?\]
skill: Describe Ohm's law and its limitations and define and describe resistance
feedback: Ohmic materials are those materials for which Ohm's law, \[V=IR\], holds true. The resistance remains the same for any change in voltage or current. The resistance of fixed resistors does not change with a change in voltage or temperature.

subject: Physics
q: When we double the voltage in a simple circuit, we double the _____.
skill: Describe Ohm's law and its limitations and define and describe resistance
feedback:
By Ohm's law, \[V=IR\], where \[V\] is the voltage, \[I\] is the current and \[R\] is the resistance.
We can see that \[V\] (voltage) and \[I\] (current) are directly proportional to each other.

So, if the voltage doubles the current also doubles (for the same resistance).

subject: Physics
q: What is the voltage across a \[6\] \[\Omega\] resistor when \[3\] A of current passes through it\[?\]
skill: Describe Ohm's law and its limitations and define and describe resistance
feedback:
We're given that R \[=6 \] \[\Omega\] and I \[=3\] A.

Using Ohm's law,

V \[=\] IR

V \[= 6\] \[\Omega \times 3 \] A

V \[= 18\] V

subject: Physics
q: The equivalent resistance in a series combination is:
options: a combination of these two formulas
skill: Describe the characteristics of series and parallel combination of resistors and solve word problems related to them
feedback:
The equivalent resistance of resistors connected in series is:

\[R_e = R_1+R_2+R_3 ....... +R_n\]

subject: Physics
q: When resistors are connected in series, the current passing through them is _____.
skill: Describe the characteristics of series and parallel combination of resistors and solve word problems related to them
feedback: In a series circuit, the current throughout the circuit is the same. In parallel circuits, the currents in the parallel branches of the circuits are different.

subject: Physics
q: The equivalent resistance of resistors in a parallel combination is _____.
options: a combination of these two formulas
skill: Describe the characteristics of series and parallel combination of resistors and solve word problems related to them
feedback:
The equivalent resistance of resistors connected in parallel is:

\[\frac{1}{R_e}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_3}....... + \frac{1}{R_e}\]

subject: Physics
q: What happens to the intensity or the brightness of the lamps connected in series as more and more lamps are added?
skill: Describe the characteristics of series and parallel combination of resistors and solve word problems related to them
feedback:
Since lamps are connected in series, the addition of more lamps increases resistance (\[R\]) in the path and reduces current (\[I\]) through each lamp. So, the brightness of the lamps decreases.

According to Ohm's law, resistance (\[R\]) and current (\[I\]) are inversely proportional to each other, so \[I = \frac {\mbox{V}}{\mbox{R}} \]

subject: Physics
q: The combined resistance of two identical resistors, connected in series, is \[8\] \[\Omega\]. Their combined resistance in a parallel arrangement will be _____.
skill: Describe the characteristics of series and parallel combination of resistors and solve word problems related to them
feedback:
We are given that \[R1\] and \[R2\] are identical.

In a series connection, \[R = R1 + R2 = 8 \ \Omega \]

\[R1\] and \[R2\] are identical, so \[ R1 = 4 \ \Omega \] and \[ R2 = 4 \ \Omega \].

In a parallel arrangement, \[\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{R1} + \frac{1}{R2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \]

\[\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{2} \]

\[R=2\] \[\Omega\]

subject: Physics
q: Watt has the same meaning as
skill: Describe electrical power and explain Joule's law and apply power equations to solve word problems
feedback: Power is measured in watts. \[1\] watt is equal to \[1\] joule of energy dissipated per second or in \[1\] second, which simply means joules per second.

subject: Physics
q: The circuit below is switched on. Calculate the amount of energy dissipated by the \[3 \ \Omega\] resistor in \[1\] minute.
skill: Describe electrical power and explain Joule's law and apply power equations to solve word problems
feedback:
We are given the resistance of the resistor and the voltage across it, as well as the time taken in minutes. We first need to convert the time into seconds. To calculate the energy dissipated by the resistor, we can use the formula of Joule's law:

\begin{align} W &= \frac{V^2t}{R} \\ \\ &= \frac{6^2 \times 60}{3} \\ \\ &= \frac{2{,}160}{3} \\ \\ &= 720 \ \mbox{ J} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: \[200\] J of energy is dissipated from a bulb in \[4\] seconds. What is the power used by the bulb?
skill: Describe electrical power and explain Joule's law and apply power equations to solve word problems
feedback:
Power is the amount of energy supplied per unit time. One of the formulas for power is:

Power \[= \frac{E}{t}\], using this formula we can calculate the power used by the bulb.

Power \[= \frac{E}{t} = \frac{200}{4} = 50\] W

subject: Physics
q: The unit of power is _____.
skill: Describe electrical power and explain Joule's law and apply power equations to solve word problems
feedback: Power is measured in watts. \[1\] watt is equal to \[1\] joule of energy dissipated per second or in \[1\] second.

subject: Physics
q: Joule's law is \[W = \]_____.
skill: Describe electrical power and explain Joule's law and apply power equations to solve word problems
feedback:
Joule's law states that the amount of heat generated in a resistance due to the flow of charges is equal to the product of the square of the electric current \[I\], resistance \[R\], and the time duration \[t\]. Mathematically this law can be written as:

\[W = I^2 Rt\], where \[W\] represent energy or heat energy.

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following currents reverses its direction?
skill: Discuss current conversion from A.C to D.C, and the types of electronic devices
feedback:
Alternating current reverses its direction. Direct current flows in only one direction.
Drive current is not a type of currents.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following types of currents is the most efficient for delivering and transmitting electricity?
skill: Discuss current conversion from A.C to D.C, and the types of electronic devices
feedback:
Alternating current is used in the transmission or delivery of electricity because it is more efficient than direct current.
Drive current is not a type of currents.

subject: Physics
q: _____ flows in only one direction and _____ reverses its direction.
skill: Discuss current conversion from A.C to D.C, and the types of electronic devices
feedback: DC, or direct current, flows in only one direction whereas AC, or alternating current, reverses its direction after every cycle. Graphically, they look like this: (note what one cycle looks like)

subject: Physics
q: AC and DC are short for _____ and _____, respectively.
skill: Discuss current conversion from A.C to D.C, and the types of electronic devices
feedback: AC is short for alternating current, and DC is short for direct current. Direct current flows in only one direction whereas alternating current reverses its direction after every cycle.

subject: Physics
q: In fixed capacitors the dielectric used is _____.
skill: Describe a capacitor and differentiate between different types of capacitors
feedback: Fixed capacitors employ the use the paper and mica as the dielectric between the plates of the capacitors.

subject: Physics
q: In  variable capacitors the dielectric used is _____.
skill: Describe a capacitor and differentiate between different types of capacitors
feedback: In variable capacitors, air is used as the dielectric. The dielectric is the material that is kept between the two plates of the capacitors.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following are examples of fixed capacitors?
skill: Describe a capacitor and differentiate between different types of capacitors
feedback: Paper and mica capacitors are both examples of fixed capacitors.

subject: Physics
q: The unit of capacitance is _____.
skill: Describe a capacitor and differentiate between different types of capacitors
feedback: Capacitance is measured in farads (F). The SI unit for capacitance is also farad.

subject: Physics
q:
LEDs are very sensitive to current. A few milliamperes of current is enough to make an LED glow; and a few hundred milliamperes is enough to damage the LED.

An LED is attached in a circuit where the input current is 200 milliamperes. How can we reduce the amount of current reaching the LED so that it doesn't get damaged?
options: by adding a capacitor to the circuit
by adding a diode to the circuit
by adding a transistor to the circuit
by adding a resistor to the circuit
skill: Identify circuit components and describe their uses in daily life
feedback:
A resistor is an electrical component that limits or regulates the flow of electrical current in an electronic circuit.
By connecting a resistor in the circuit, the current will be reduced to make sure the LED is not damaged.

subject: Physics
q: Capacitance of a capacitor is the measure of its ability to _____.
skill: Identify circuit components and describe their uses in daily life
feedback:
The ability of a capacitor to store charge is known as capacitance.
The unit of capacitance is Farad.

subject: Physics
q: An integrated circuit or a microchip contains the following components:
skill: Identify circuit components and describe their uses in daily life
feedback: A microchip contains many complete circuits that contain resistors, transistors, capacitors, and diodes.

subject: Physics
q: Ali is taking a flash photograph and needs his camera to produce a huge burst of light in a fraction of a second. Which of these will help him do that?
skill: Identify circuit components and describe their uses in daily life
feedback: A capacitor generally releases its energy much more rapidly-often in seconds or less. So, a capacitor attached to the flash gun charges up for a few seconds using energy from the camera's batteries. Once the capacitor is fully charged, it can release all that energy in an instant through the flash bulb.

subject: Physics
q: The symbol of a diode is _____.
skill: Identify circuit components and describe their uses in daily life
feedback: A diode is a semiconductor device with two terminals. Diodes generally allow the flow of current in one direction. The symbol for diodes is:

subject: Physics
q: Identify a danger of electricity with reference to the image below:
options: Flying a kite in an open field can cause a short circuit in the electricity line.
The house is located dangerously close to the transformer.
Flying a kite in an open field can cause overheating of the power lines.
Flying a kite too close to the power lines can result in an electric shock.
skill: Describe the hazards of electricity and explain safety measures including neutral and earth wires
feedback:
Flying a kite too close to the power lines may cause an electric shock.
The metallic wire of the kite is a conductor. If the kite touches the power line, high current may flow down the wire through the person and into the ground, thus completing a circuit and causing an electric shock.

subject: Physics
q: The wire which is maintained at zero potential is the _____.
skill: Describe the hazards of electricity and explain safety measures including neutral and earth wires
feedback: The neutral wire is maintained at zero potential. The neutral wire is a return wire that completes the circuit and is connected to the ground.

subject: Physics
q: In diagrams of electromagnetism, a cross shows the direction of the current is _____ .
options: top to bottom and into the paper
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback: In diagrams that show wires going inwards or outwards of the page, crosses are used to show current flowing into the page and dots are used to show current flowing out of the page.

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic lines will be in the form of concentric circles if the conductor is _____.
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback: The magnetic lines produced by the current flowing through a straight conductor are in concentric circles around the conductor. Concentric circles means circles inside other circles.

subject: Physics
q: The number of lines of force in a magnetic field depends upon _____.
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback: The stronger a magnetic field is, the more lines of force it has. The shape and size of a coil does not affect the number of field lines, neither does the direction of the current. The direction of the current only affects the direction of the lines, not their number.

subject: Physics
q: Which statement is true about magnetic poles\[?\]
options: Magnetic poles do not affect each other.
A single magnetic pole does not exist.
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback: Magnetic poles cannot be separated. It is not possible to have a magnetic north pole without a magnetic south pole and vice versa. So, a single magnetic pole does not exist.

subject: Physics
q: What is the direction of the magnetic field lines inside a bar magnet\[?\]
options: from the south pole to the north pole
from the north pole to the south pole
There are no magnetic field lines.
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback: The magnetic field lines produced inside a bar magnet are in the direction from the north pole to the south pole. We can find the direction of magnetic field lines with the help of the right-hand grip rule.

subject: Physics
q: The presence of a magnetic field can be detected by a:
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback: When a current passes through a conductor, it produces a magnetic field around the conductor and we can detect this magnetic field by using a magnetic compass.

subject: Physics
q: According to the right-hand rule for a current-carrying conductor, the current will be in the _____.
topic: The magnetic effect of a steady current
skill: Demonstrate that an electric current in a conductor produces a magnetic field around it
feedback:
The right-hand rule for a current-carrying conductor states:
"Grasp a wire with your right hand such that your thumb is pointed in the direction of current. Then, the curling fingers of your hand will point in the direction of the magnetic field."

subject: Physics
q: The coils of a transformer are usually wound around a/an _____ core.
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback: Transformers work on the principle of mutual induction. In this, an emf or current is induced in a conductive coil, due to a change in current in a neighbouring coil. This change in current creates a changing magnetic field. When the changing magnetic field lines cross the other coil, a current is induced in it. This happens due to the magnetic link between the two coils, termed as magnetic coupling or inductive coupling. This coupling can be increased by inserting a magnetic core, like one made of iron, inside the coils, in order to increase the magnetic field lines and make the magnetic field stronger.

subject: Physics
q: What is the working principle of transformers?
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
A transformer is an electrical device that is used to increase or decrease AC voltages through the process of mutual induction. In this process, an emf or current is induced in a conductor, due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor.

Self-induction is the production of a voltage or an emf across a current-carrying conductor itself, due to a change in current in it.
Electrostatic induction is a method of electrically charging a conductor by bringing a charged object close to it.
Conduction is a method of electrically charging a conductor by touching it with a charged object.

subject: Physics
q:
Coil \[X\] is connected with a galvanometer and placed close to coil \[Y\], which is connected with a battery through a switch. Closing the switch causes the needle of the galvanometer to deflect momentarily. The same is observed when the switch is opened.
Which of the following statements is CORRECT about the reason and direction of movement of the needle, upon OPENING the switch?
options: The number of magnetic field lines passing through \[X\] increases, and the induced current is in the same direction as before.
The number of magnetic field lines passing through \[X\] decreases, and the induced current is in the opposite direction.
The number of magnetic field lines passing through \[X\] increases, and the induced current is in the opposite direction.
The number of magnetic field lines passing through \[X\] decreases, and the induced current is in the same direction as before.
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
The deflection of the needle of the galvanometer indicates current flow and direction. When the switch of coil \[Y\] is closed, a current begins to flow through the coil, causing a magnetic field to develop across it. Some of these magnetic field lines pass through coil \[X\]. As long as the current passing through \[Y\] changes, the magnetic field lines crossing through \[X\] also change. As a result, a current is induced in coil \[X\], causing the needle of the galvanometer to deflect. The deflection of the needle is momentary because the development of and change in current in coil \[Y\] happens only for a fraction of a second as the switch is closed. Eventually, the current becomes steady, causing the number of magnetic field lines crossing \[X\] to become constant. No change in the number of lines causes the induced current (and hence, the deflection of the needle) to disappear.

Similarly, when the switch of coil \[Y\] is opened, the flow of the current through \[Y\] initially decreases and then stops. The decrease in current, again, causes the number of magnetic field lines crossing \[X\] to change (decrease). Therefore, a current is momentarily induced in the opposite direction in coil \[X\].

subject: Physics
q: What are transformers used for?
options: converting electrical energy into mechanical energy
converting AC voltages to DC voltages
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
A transformer is an electrical device that is used to increase or decrease AC voltages through the process of mutual induction.

Generators produce electricity when a coil is rotated in a magnetic field.
Motors produce mechanical (rotational kinetic) energy from electrical energy in a current-carrying coil placed inside a magnetic field. Finally, the conversion of AC to DC is done by devices called rectifiers.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT a condition for mutual induction to take place?
options: One of the conductors should create a changing magnetic field around itself.
The conductors should be electrically linked with each other.
The conductors should be present in each other's magnetic fields.
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
Mutual induction is the production of an emf in a conductor, due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor. This change in current creates a changing magnetic field. When the changing magnetic field lines cross the other conductor, a current is induced in it. This can only happen if there is a magnetic link between the two conductors. This is termed as magnetic coupling or inductive coupling.
The conductors should NOT be electrically connected with each other.

subject: Physics
q: Coil \[X\] is connected with a galvanometer and placed close to coil \[Y\], which is connected with a battery through a switch. Closing or opening the switch causes the needle of the galvanometer to deflect momentarily. Which phenomenon does this observation indicate?
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
A galvanometer is a sensitive instrument used for detecting the presence and direction of an electric current. When the switch of coil \[Y\] is closed, a current begins to flow through the coil, causing a magnetic field to develop across the it. Some of these magnetic field lines pass through coil \[X\] and induce a current in it. As opening and closing the switch causes a change in the current passing through \[Y\], the magnetic field lines crossing through \[X\] also change. As a result, a current is induced in coil \[X\], causing the needle of the galvanometer to deflect.
Additional explanation:

This phenomenon is known as mutual induction, in which, an emf or current is induced in a conductor, due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor.

The deflection of the needle is momentary because the development of and change in current in coil \[Y\] happens only for a fraction of a second as the switch is closed. Eventually, the current becomes steady, causing the number of magnetic field lines crossing \[X\] to become constant. No change in the number of lines causes the induced current to disappear.

Self-induction is the production of a voltage or emf across a current-carrying conductor itself, due to a change in current in it.
Electrostatic induction is a method of electrically charging a conductor by bringing a charged object near it.
Conduction is a method of electrically charging a conductor by touching it with a charged object.

subject: Physics
q: What is self-induction?
options: the production of an electromotive force (emf) across a current-carrying conductor, due to a change in the current in the same conductor
the production of kinetic energy in a current-carrying conductor, placed inside a magnetic field
the production of kinetic energy in a conductor, due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor
the production of an electromotive force (emf) across a conductor, due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
When a current passes through a conductor, a magnetic field develops across it. When the magnetic field lines change due to a change in the current, it induces a voltage in the same conductor. This voltage or emf is also called back emf, since it opposes the applied voltage. This production of emf across a current-carrying conductor itself, due to a change in current in it is called self-induction.

The production of an emf or current in a conductor, due to a change in current in the neighbouring conductor is called mutual induction.
The production of (rotational) kinetic energy in a current-carrying conductor (loop), placed inside a magnetic field is the working principle of motors.

subject: Physics
q: What is mutual induction?
options: the production of an electromotive force (emf) across a current-carrying conductor, due to a change in current in the same conductor
the production of kinetic energy in a current-carrying conductor placed inside a magnetic field
the production of kinetic energy in a conductor due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor
the production of an electromotive force (emf) across a conductor due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor
skill: Describe self and mutual induction and explain how a transformer works and its purpose in AC circuits
feedback:
When a current passes through a conductor, a magnetic field develops across it. If another conductor is placed within its vicinity, some of the magnetic field lines will pass through it. As these magnetic field lines passing through the conductor change (due changes in current in the neighbouring conductor), a current is induced in it because of the production of an induced electromotive force (emf). This production of an emf or current across an electrical conductor, due to a change in current in a neighbouring conductor is called mutual induction.

The production of voltage across a current-carrying conductor itself, due to a change in current in it is called self-induction.
The production of (rotational) kinetic energy in a current-carrying conductor (loop) placed inside a magnetic field is the working principle of motors.

subject: Physics
q: An anode is the _____ electrode of a circuit or a battery.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: All cells and batteries have electrodes. The positive electrode is called the anode, while the negative electrode is called the cathode.

subject: Physics
q: How is the tungsten filament heated in an electron gun in order to produce thermionic emission?
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback:
Thermionic emission is the process by which a hot metal surface emits electrons. All metals have free electrons. These electrons stay with the metal at room temperature due to the attraction with the positively charged protons in the nuclei of the metal atoms. When the metal is heated, by any means, these electrons get enough energy to break free from the attractive forces of the nuclei. This results in the metal emitting electrons.
In an electron gun, thermionic emission is produced by electrically heating a fine tungsten filament. Normally, a voltage of \[6\] V with a current of \[0.3\] A is enough to achieve this.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT a component of an electron gun?
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback:
An electron gun is used to produce an electron beam through thermionic emission from a fine tungsten filament (also its cathode), when heated electrically. Using a high voltage supply, a high positive potential is applied to a cylindrical anode, in order to accelerate the electrons to a high speed. These electrons then pass through a tiny hole in the anode, in the form of a fine beam.
A cylindrical cathode is not a component of an electron gun. The cathode, in this setup, is the tungsten filament itself, which produces the electrons.

subject: Physics
q: How much voltage and current are usually supplied to a tungsten filament to produce thermionic emission in an electron gun?
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback:
Thermionic emission is the process by which a hot metal surface emits electrons. All metals have free electrons. These electrons stay with the metal at room temperature due to the attraction with the positively charged protons in the nuclei of the metal atoms. When the metal is heated, these electrons get enough energy to break free from the attractive forces of the nuclei. This results in the metal emitting electrons.
In an electron gun, thermionic emission is produced by electrically heating a fine tungsten filament. Normally, a voltage of \[\bf 6\] V with a current of \[\bf 0.3\] A is enough to achieve this.

subject: Physics
q: How is a beam of electrons affected by an electric field?
options: It is not affected by an electric field.
It is deflected towards the positive plate.
It is deflected towards the negative plate.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback:
When a beam of electrons passes through an electric field created by two parallel metal plates that have a potential difference between them, the electron beam is deflected towards the positive plate.
This is because electrons are negatively charged, and hence, are attracted by the positive plate and repelled by the negative one.
The stronger the electric field, the greater the deflection, and vice versa.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following materials is used to make the electron emitter in an electron gun?
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: Electron guns are made with a tungsten filament that emits the electrons when it is heated. The electrons pass through an anode which turns the flow of electrons in the ray into fine and sharp beams.

subject: Physics
q: Metals contain a large number of free electrons. What happens to those electrons during thermionic emission?
options: The electrons produce heat energy as emissions.
The metal gains thermionic emissions and the electrons vibrate.
The electrons stick to the metal and restrict emissions.
The electrons gain enough energy to escape the metal as emissions.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: All metals contain a sea of electrons. These are free electrons. But at room temperature, these electrons do not have the energy to break free from the attractive forces of the nucleus. When the metal is heated, these electrons get enough energy to break free from the metal. This process is called thermionic emissions.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT?
options: The heated filament of an electron gun emits the electrons, not the anode.
The heated filament of an electron gun requires a very high voltage to emit electrons.
The heated filament of an electron gun is usually made of tungsten.
The anode of an electron gun is supplied with a very high voltage.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: The heated filament in an electron gun is typically provided voltage around 6 V, which is not too high. Comparatively, the anode in an electron gun is supplied with a very high voltage which goes into thousands of volts.

subject: Physics
q: The power supplied to the filament supply of the electron gun is _____ than the power supplied to the anode.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: The filament supply of an electron gun, which is also its cathode, is usually a 6 V battery. However, the power supplied to the anode has a very high voltage (in thousands of volts). This helps accelerate the electrons and lets them pass through the anode in the form of fine and sharp beams.

subject: Physics
q: Which part of an electron gun emits the rays of electrons?
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback:
The rays of electrons in an electron gun are emitted by the heated filament. It is usually made of tungsten, and the metal filament releases electrons as it heats up.

The anode guides the electrons and turns them into fine and sharp beams. The cathode rays themselves are the rays of electrons, and they are not a part of the electron gun.

subject: Physics
q: The deflection of cathode rays in electric and magnetic fields show that the cathode rays ______.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: In 1897, J.J. Thomson carried experiments to observe the deflection of cathode rays in electric and magnetic fields. The way the rays deflected in these fields showed that the rays contained negatively charged particles. These particles were given the name of electrons.

subject: Physics
q: The rays emitted by cathodes during thermionic emissions have particles which are _____.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: Cathodes are the negative electrodes of a circuit or battery. The negative electrodes (cathodes) emit rays which are negatively charged because of the presence of negatively charged particles.

subject: Physics
q: A cathode is the _____ electrode of a circuit or battery.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: All cells and batteries have electrodes. The negative electrode is called the cathode, and the positive electrode is called the anode.

subject: Physics
q: Which particles are emitted from the surface of a hot cathode?
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: Cathodes are negatively charged electrodes and anodes are positively charged electrodes. The hot surface of a negatively charged cathode releases the negatively charged particles: electrons.

subject: Physics
q: The process by which a hot metal surface emits electrons is called _____.
skill: Explain thermionic emission and describe the simple construction and use of an electron gun as a source of electron beam - Physics
feedback: All metals have free electrons. These electrons stay with the metal at room temperature due to the attraction with the positively charged protons in the nucleus. When the metal is heated, these electrons get enough energy to break free from the attractive forces from the nuclei. This results in the metal emitting electrons when it is heated, and this emission is called thermionic emission.

subject: Physics
q: What material is the fluorescent screen of a CRO made up of?
skill: Describe the basic principle of CRO and make a list of its uses
feedback:
The fluorescent screen of a CRO is made up of phosphor, which glows when hit by an electron. This way, we see an accurate result of where the electron beams hit the screen.

Note: Phosphorus is an element from the periodic table and should not be confused with phosphor (a material that glows under certain conditions).

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following can be used as a brightness control in CROs?
options: grid G of the electron gun
skill: Describe the basic principle of CRO and make a list of its uses
feedback: Grid G, which is a part of the electron gun, in CROs is a negatively charged electrode. By adjusting the negative potential of this grid electrode, we can control how many electrons pass through it. This adjusts the brightness of the electron beam.

subject: Physics
q: The electron gun of a CRO consists of an electrically heated cathode, a negative electrode called _____, and a focussing anode.
skill: Describe the basic principle of CRO and make a list of its uses
feedback: The electron gun component of a CRO has its own smaller components. These include the heated metal cathode that ejects the electrons, a negatively charged grid called grid G, which increases or reduces the number of electrons passing through it, and an anode which sharpens the electron beam.

subject: Physics
q: A cathode-ray oscilloscope contains which of the following components?
skill: Describe the basic principle of CRO and make a list of its uses
feedback:
A cathode-ray oscilloscope contains the following components:

1) An electron gun
2) A deflection system
3) A fluorescent screen

subject: Physics
q: Cathode-ray tubes are used in which of the following equipment?
skill: Describe the basic principle of CRO and make a list of its uses
feedback: Cathode-ray tubes guide and deflect electrons to produce an image on a screen. This usage of CRTs is applied in televisions.

subject: Physics
q: The screen of a cathode ray tube consists of a thin layer of _____.
skill: Describe the basic principle of CRO and make a list of its uses
feedback:
The screen of a cathode-ray tube, which is a fluorescent screen, consists of a thin layer of phosphorus.
Phosphorous is a material that gives light when it is bombarded by fast moving electrons.

subject: Physics
q: If digital signals are easy to transmit without much loss and can also be modified if needed, why are they converted into analogue signals after they have been processed?
options: Analogue signals are clearer than digital signals.
After they have been processed, digital signals lose their signal strength and become weak.
Digital signals cannot be perceived by human senses.
Digital signals are more expensive to transmit as compared to analogue signals.
skill: Explain the importance of electronics and differentiate between analogue and digital electronics
feedback:
Human senses can only perceive signals that are continuous. For example, the sound we perceive by our ears is continuous, and not collected in discrete steps.
Digital signals are discontinuous and discrete, and therefore cannot be perceived by human senses. Because human senses can perceive only continuous, analogue signals, digital signals have to be converted into analogue signals after they have been processed and/or modified. This is achieved through digital-to-analogue converters or DACs.

subject: Physics
q: What do the blocks \[X\] and \[Y\] in the following diagram represent, respectively?
skill: Explain the importance of electronics and differentiate between analogue and digital electronics
feedback:
A continuously varying sound signal from the source is converted by the mic into a continuously varying electric current; an analogue signal. As this needs to be fed to a computer, which understands only binary or discrete values, it must be converted into a digital signal.
The loudspeaker is an analogue device, so the digital signal from the computer must be converted back into analogue.

Therefore, block \[X\] represents an analogue-to-digital converter, ADC,
while block \[Y\] represents a digital-to-analogue converter, DAC (specifically, an audio DAC in this case).

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following are analogue quantities?
skill: Explain the importance of electronics and differentiate between analogue and digital electronics
feedback: All of these are analogue quantities. This is because these quantities vary continuously and not suddenly in discrete steps.

subject: Physics
q: Quantities whose values vary continuously and not in discrete steps are called _____.
skill: Explain the importance of electronics and differentiate between analogue and digital electronics
feedback:
Quantities whose values vary continuously and not in discrete steps are called analogue quantities. The opposite is true for digital quantities. Digital quantities vary in discrete steps instead of continuous variations.

An example of an analogue quantity is time. When time changes, it does not jump from \[1\] second to \[2\] seconds. Instead, it goes through \[1.01\] seconds to \[1.02\] and so on till \[1.99\] and then to \[2\] seconds. In fact, we can go even deeper in milliseconds and nanoseconds.

Digital quantities work in discrete steps. For instance, a computer is either on or off. It is either at \[0\] or \[1\]. It cannot be \[0.5\] on and \[0.5\] off.

subject: Physics
q: Electronics is a branch of physics and technology that deals with circuits, their components, and _____.
options: the energy generated by these circuits
the heat generated by these circuits
the movement of electrons in these
skill: Explain the importance of electronics and differentiate between analogue and digital electronics
feedback: As the name suggests, ELECTRONics is a branch of physics that studies and designs the movement of electrons through circuits designed to carry out different tasks.

subject: Physics
q:
Given that,

\[ X = \overline{A \cdot B}\]

What is \[X\] if \[A = 1\]?
options: Need to know the value of \[B\]
skill: State the basic operations of digital electronics and identify, draw, and state the actions of symbols for the logic gates
feedback: From the equation we can see that \[X\] is the result of a NAND operation on \[A\] and \[B\]. Given is the truth table for an AND gate and a NAND gate. In an AND operation, if \[A\] AND \[B\] are \[1\] then the answer \[X\] is \[1\], otherwise it is \[0\]. In a NAND gate, these results are reversed. We only know the value of \[A\], which is \[1\]. If \[B = 1\] then \[X = 0\] but if \[B = 0\] then \[X = 1\]. Look at the table and confirm it.

subject: Physics
q: In case of OR and AND operations, if switches \[S_{1}\] and \[S_{2}\] are both open then the lamp is _____.
skill: State the basic operations of digital electronics and identify, draw, and state the actions of symbols for the logic gates
feedback:
In case of OR operation, the lamp will be on if at least one of the switches is closed.

In case of AND operation, the lamp will be on when both switches are closed.

In this case, both switches \[S_{1}\] and \[S_{2}\] are open, so the lamp is off for both AND and OR operations.

subject: Physics
q: AND gate can be formed by using two
skill: State the basic operations of digital electronics and identify, draw, and state the actions of symbols for the logic gates
feedback:
NAND and NOR gates are universal gates as any kind of logic gate can be obtained by using combinations of these. To get an AND gate we can use two NAND gates.

The output of the first NAND gate is given to the second NAND gate as inputs on both of its terminals. Now, the output of the second NAND gate will be:

x \[=\overline{\overline{A.B}} = A.B\]

subject: Physics
q: An inverter is another name for _____ gate.
