skill: State the basic operations of digital electronics and identify, draw, and state the actions of symbols for the logic gates
feedback:
An inverter is another name for the NOT gate.

NOT gates perform the basic logical function called inversion, which converts one logical level into the opposite logical level.

subject: Physics
q: In a CBIS, data CANNOT be stored in which of the following forms?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Data is facts and figures that are used by programs to produce useful information. It can be in the form of text, audio, video, etc. The patterns of data can be converted into electrical signals, so that they can be transmitted, but data cannot actually be stored in the form of electricity. It is stored in devices like magnetic or optical disks that do not require electricity to store the data that has been inserted into them.

subject: Physics
q:
What is Information and Communication Technology (ICT) concerned with?
I.   creation of data II.  processing of data III. storage of data IV. transmission of data
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback:
Information and Communication Technology (ICT) combines the fields of Information Technology (IT) and telecommunication.
Information technology concerns the storage of information for further use and transmission. Telecommunication is the method used to communicate or transmit information to distant places.
However, it is only after the data has been processed that it can be used for storage or transmission.

subject: Physics
q:
An audio signal is recorded through a mic. It is then edited in a computer to remove background noise. Which components of a CBIS are involved in this process?
I.   hardware
II.  software
III. people
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: In this example, the mic is the hardware component, which is used as an input device to record the audio signal, which is the data \[-\] also a component of a CBIS. The computer programme which is used for noise removal from the audio signal is the software component of the CBIS. Since the recording or the editing cannot be performed without a person doing it, people are also involved.

subject: Physics
q:
In what form are software programs and data stored inside a computer?
I.   analogue
II.  digital
III. machine-readable
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Software programs and data are stored inside a computer in machine-readable form. Machine-readable form simply means a language that computers operate in. This language is binary \[- 0\]'s and \[1\]'s. In other words, machine-readable form is digital. Analogue signals cannot be processed by a computer.

subject: Physics
q: What is the computer terminology for 'processed data'?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: The computer terminology for 'processed data' is information. For a piece of data to be fit for processing by a computer, it has to be in digital form, since that is the language a computer understands. After processing, the data is converted into useful information which is transmitted over communication channels to far off places.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is the most important component of CBIS?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: People are the most important component of CBIS. Although all components are critically important, people are the ones who build the hardware, create the software, create and input the data, and write the procedures. Finally, people are the ones who use the whole system so without people, there is no CBIS.

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following options is a representation of the data component of a CBIS?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback:
A video is just data in graphic form. This means a video is just information presented in visual or video form.
A schoolbook also has data but it is not a part of CBIS.
A computer game comes under the software component of CBIS as it is a program.
A camera comes under the hardware component of CBIS. It creates data (video or photograph).

subject: Physics
q: Data is stored on the memory or disk/tape. Software programs are stored on _____.
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Software programs are stored on the disks of computers. The CPU accesses them when it needs to run a software to complete a task. For instance, the software program Microsoft Word is installed on the disk of a computer. When we open it on our screens, the CPU accesses the program from the computer disk and opens it up for us to use.

subject: Physics
q: After taking a picture with a camera, it is edited and perfected on a computer before it is printed. Which component of a CBIS carries out the editing of the picture?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: A software carries out the editing operations on the picture taken by the camera. In this example, the camera is the hardware component, which is used as an input device, the picture is the data, and the computer programme which is used to edit the picture is the software component of the CBIS.

subject: Physics
q: Input devices of a CBIS fall under which of the following components?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Input devices provide the information to a CBIS to store or analyse. This information is given through input devices such as keyboards, mouses, and cameras etc.

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following is not a component of Computer Based Information Systems (CBIS)?
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback:
PC is the short form of 'Personal Computer' which means that it is a whole computer. A computer is a CBIS, not a component of a CBIS.
People are a part of CBIS as they are the ones which provide, and then use, the information given to a Computer Based Information System.

subject: Physics
q: ______ are used to transmit information to our televisions and radios.
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Radio waves are low frequency electromagnetic waves that carry information to our televisions and radios. The televisions and radios receive that signal, convert it into light and/or sound signals, which our senses then perceive. Our eyes can see the light from the TV screens and our ears can hear the sounds made by the speakers, but our body cannot perceive this information when it is in radio wave form.

subject: Physics
q: Satellites use _____ to send and receive information.
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Satellites use microwaves to communicate with each other and to send information back to the Earth’s surface. This is because microwaves are not reflected by the atmosphere and can easily penetrate it. Radio waves, which are used to transmit radio and television signals, reflect off the ionosphere, which is one of the higher layers of our atmosphere, because they cannot easily penetrate that layer.

subject: Physics
q: Our cell phones use _____ to transmit information wirelessly.
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback: Cell phones, or cellular mobile phones, use microwaves to send and receive information (calls, texts, emails). Microwaves are used in radars and to transmit information farther into space—to satellites and spaceships. Radio waves also play a role in cell phone communication. While cell phones use microwaves to carry information to nearby cell phone towers, cell phone towers then send radio waves to communicate with other cell phone towers, which are further away.

subject: Physics
q: All wireless modern telecommunications use some kind of _____.
skill: Describe information technology (IT) and the components of information technology
feedback:
Modern wireless telecommunications make use of electromagnetic waves to transfer or transmit information. What is important to understand here is that every form of communication that we have is a transfer of information. When you text or call a friend, both of you exchange information with each other. Even saying "hello, hi" is sharing information.

Electromagnetic waves carry this information over long distances without the use of wires.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following DOES NOT represent a pair of different isotopes?
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback:
Isotopes are the atoms of an element that have the same number of protons as each other but a different number of neutrons. This means that their atomic number \[Z\] is the same but their atomic mass number \[A\] is different from each other.

Both atoms in all of the given pairs have the same atomic number but different mass numbers, except for \[_{1}^{2} X_i\], \[_{1}^{2} X_{ii}\], in which both atoms have the same atomic number as well as the same mass number. Hence, it does not represent a pair of different isotopes but \[2\] same atoms of 'deuterium', which is an isotope of hydrogen.

subject: Physics
q: The neutron number \[N\] of an atom is the number of _____ in that atom.
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback:
The neutron number \[N\], as the name suggests, is the number of neutrons that an atom has. You can calculate it by subtracting the atomic number \[Z\] from the atomic mass number \[A\].

\[N = A - Z\]

subject: Physics
q: What is the atomic number \[Z\] of an atom?
options: It is the number of electrons of an atom.
It is the number of neutrons of an atom.
It is the number of nucleons of an atom.
It is the number of protons of an atom.
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback: The atomic number \[Z\] of an atom is the number of protons that that atom has. For instance the proton number \[Z\] of Oxygen \[O\] is \[8\] as it has \[8\] protons.

subject: Physics
q: According to Rutherford's nuclear model the mass of an atom is almost equal to _____.
options: the mass of the electrons plus the mass of the protons
the mass of the protons plus the mass of the neutrons
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback: Rutherford discovered that almost all of the mass of an atom is concentrated in the centre; the nucleus. This is because the mass of a proton is about \[1{,}836\] times the mass of an electron while a neutron weighs almost the same as a proton. The protons and neutrons are in the nucleus of an atom while the electrons are orbiting outside the nucleus. This means that almost all of the mass of an atom is inside the nucleus.

subject: Physics
q: The isotope \[_1^1\]H contains _____.
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback: \[_1^1\]H is an isotope of hydrogen called protium. It contains one proton in the nucleus and one electron that revolves around the nucleus. It has no neutrons.

subject: Physics
q: One of the isotopes of uranium is \[_{92}^{238}\mbox{U}\]. The number of neutrons in this isotope is_____.
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback:
We are given that the number of nucleons \[= 238\] and the number of protons \[ = 92\].

Number of neutrons \[ = \] Number of nucleons (protons + neutrons) \[-\] Number of protons

Number of neutrons \[ = 238 - 92 = 146 \]

So, there are 146 neutrons in \[_{92}^{238}\mbox{U}\].

subject: Physics
q: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with a different _____.
skill: Describe Rutherford's nuclear model and show nuclides with the symbol of proton number(z), nuclear number (a), and nuclide notation(x)
feedback: Isotopes are atoms of an element which have the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons in their nuclei. Isotopes of an element have the same atomic number but different atomic masses.

subject: Physics
q:
When projected perpendicularly into electric and magnetic fields, which of the following radiations is/are deflected?
I.   alpha (\[\alpha\]) particles
II.  beta (\[\beta\]) particles
III. gamma (\[\gamma\]) rays
skill: Describe and explain the three types of radiations and their nature and properties - Physics
feedback:
Both alpha (\[\alpha\]) and beta (\[\beta\]) radiations are charged particles. Hence, when projected perpendicularly into electric fields, alpha particles \[-\] because they're positively charged \[-\] are deflected towards the negative plate, whereas beta particles \[-\] because they're negatively charged \[-\] are deflected towards the positive plate.
In magnetic fields, alpha and beta particles are deflected in opposite directions.

Since gamma (\[\gamma\]) rays are uncharged or electrically neutral, they're not deflected by electric and magnetic fields.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following kinds of radiation has the most penetrating ability?
skill: Describe and explain the three types of radiations and their nature and properties - Physics
feedback:
Radiations have the following penetrating abilities:

Alpha (\[\alpha\]) particles are least penetrative. They are relatively large and heavy particles which hit and interact with other particles easily. That's why they are blocked more easily and are least penetrative as radiations.

Beta (\[\beta\]) particles are relatively small particles (electrons). They have a higher penetrative ability than alpha particles but are not as penetrative as gamma rays.

Gamma (\[\gamma\]) rays have a small wavelength and high energy, and are high frequency radiations. Gamma radiations can penetrate almost all substances except lead.

X-rays are quite penetrating as well, but since they have a greater wavelength than that of gamma rays, they're still less penetrating comparatively.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following kinds of radiation has the largest mass?
skill: Describe and explain the three types of radiations and their nature and properties - Physics
feedback:
Below is a list of atomic radiations and a description of their masses: Alpha (\[\alpha\]) particles consist of \[2\] protons and \[2\] neutrons just like a helium nucleus. They have the largest mass.
Beta (\[\beta\]) particles consist of a stream of high energy electrons. Electrons are lighter than a helium nucleus.
Gamma (\[\gamma\]) rays are electromagnetic waves which have negligible mass just like light rays.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following types of radiation are emitted by radioactive materials?
skill: Describe and explain the three types of radiations and their nature and properties - Physics
feedback:
Radioactive materials usually emit these radiations:

alpha (\[\alpha\]) particles
beta (\[\beta\]) particles
gamma (\[\gamma\]) rays

subject: Physics
q: What happens to the atomic number of an element which emits one alpha particle and a beta particle?
skill: Explain nuclear transmutations and the meaning of half-life of a radioactive material
feedback:
When an element emits one alpha particle and a beta particle, its atomic number decreases by \[2\].

In alpha decay, a helium nucleus is emitted. It consists of two protons and two neutrons, resulting in a reduction of \[2\] in the atomic number.
The beta particle is actually an electron and it does not affect the atomic number.

subject: Physics
q: The charge on alpha particles is _____.
skill: Explain nuclear transmutations and the meaning of half-life of a radioactive material
feedback: An alpha particle is a helium nucleus comprising two protons and two neutrons with a charge of \[+2e\].

q: Which of the following statements is FALSE about radioisotopes?
options: Radioactive tracers can be used to trace leaks in underground pipes.
Radioisotopes are used to check the function and efficiency of a fertiliser.
All radioisotopes can be used to estimate the age of a fossil.
Radioisotopes are isotopes of an element with unstable nuclei.
skill: Describe radio isotopes and their uses and explain the process of carbon dating
feedback:
All radioisotopes can be used to estimate the age of a fossil is WRONG.

Not all radioisotopes can be used to estimate the age of a fossil. This is because there are a lot of elements that are not naturally found in living things.

Carbon is found in almost every living organism and organic compound. Therefore, C-\[14\] isotope of carbon is usually used to estimate the age of a fossil.

subject: Physics
q: What enables the use of carbon-dating to find out the age of an organism or fossil?
options: C-\[14\] is the most abundant isotope of carbon and is the major component of all living things.
Our atmosphere is full of C-\[14\] and therefore all living things are highly radioactive.
After a plant or an animal dies, it stops taking in fresh C-\[14\] and the C-\[14\] present in it starts decaying.
When plants or animals die, they start absorbing fresh C-\[14\] from the atmosphere and the soil.
skill: Describe radio isotopes and their uses and explain the process of carbon dating
feedback:
Carbon dating is the process of calculating or estimating when an organism was last alive, by using the half-life of the carbon-\[14\] isotope.

All organisms absorb a small amount of the C-\[14\] isotope of carbon in the form of carbon dioxide or other organic compounds. When an organism dies, it stops absorbing fresh C-\[14\]. C-\[14\], being a radioactive isotope, starts decaying.
By comparing the amount of C-\[14\] left in a dead organism to that present in a living organism, the age of that fossil can be calculated.

subject: Physics
q: What is carbon-\[14\] dating used for?
options: to determine when an organism was last alive
to calculate the half-life of carbon-\[14\]
to explore metabolism in human and animals
to reduce the speed of neutrons in nuclear reactors
skill: Describe radio isotopes and their uses and explain the process of carbon dating
feedback:
Carbon-\[14\] dating or carbon dating is the process of calculating or estimating the age of a fossil, by using the rate of decay or half-life of carbon-\[14\] isotope.

All organisms absorb a small amount of C-\[14\] isotope of carbon in the form of carbon dioxide or other organic compounds. When an organism dies, it stops absorbing fresh C-\[14\]. Carbon-\[14\], being a radioactive isotope, starts decaying and converting into a stable element.
By comparing the amount of C-\[14\] in a dead organism to that of it in a living organism, it can be calculated when that organism was last alive on earth as we can compare it with the half-life of C-\[14\].

subject: Physics
q: What are radioisotopes?
options: atoms of an element with the same mass number but different atomic numbers
isotopes of an element that are radioactive due to unstable nuclei
isotopes of an element that have an equal number of protons and neutrons
atoms of an element that have zero energy in their nuclei
skill: Describe radio isotopes and their uses and explain the process of carbon dating
feedback:
Radioisotopes, also known as radioactive isotopes, are isotopes of an element that are radioactive.
These isotopes become radioactive due to the instability of their nuclei or excess energy in their nuclei. When the number of protons or the number of neutrons in a nucleus is very high compared to the other particles, the nucleus becomes unstable and releases the excess protons or neutrons in the form of radiations, in order to become stable. This results in the phenomenon of radioactivity.

subject: Physics
q: Which radio isotope is used for curing cancerous tumours\[?\]
skill: Describe radio isotopes and their uses and explain the process of carbon dating
feedback: Radio isotopes are used in nuclear medicines for curing various diseases. Cobalt - \[60\] is a radio isotope that is used  to cure cancerous tumours and cells. The radiation kills the cells of the malignant tumour in the patient.

subject: Physics
q: Electronvolt (eV) is a unit of energy used in atomic and nuclear physics. \[1\] eV \[=\] _____.
skill: Describe the processes and practical applications of fission and fusion
feedback: \[1\] electronvolt is the amount of energy acquired by an electron as it travels across a potential difference of \[1\] volt. Therefore, \[1\] eV \[=1.6 \times 10^{-19}\] joules.

subject: Physics
q: What happens in a nuclear fusion reaction?
options: two or more light molecules combine to form one heavy compound
one heavy nucleus splits into two or more lighter or smaller nuclei
two or more light nuclei combine to form one heavy nucleus
one heavy molecule splits into two or more atoms
skill: Describe the processes and practical applications of fission and fusion
feedback:
In nuclear fusion reactions, two or more small nuclei combine or fuse together to form one heavy nucleus. For example:

\[^{2}_{1}H + ^{3}_{1}H ~ \rightarrow ~ ^{4}_{2} He + ^1_0n + \mbox{energy}\]
Two isotopes of hydrogen, deuterium and tritium, fuse together to form helium which releases a neutron (shown in the equation with \[^1_0n\]) and energy. The mass of the product nucleus is always less than the collective mass of the original nuclei. Where does that remaining mass go? It is converted into energy; lots of energy; nuclear energy.

subject: Physics
q: When a heavy nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei, the process _____.
skill: Describe the processes and practical applications of fission and fusion
feedback: In nuclear fission, when a heavy nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei, a large amount of nuclear energy is released.

subject: Physics
q: The release of energy by the sun is due to _____.
skill: Describe the processes and practical applications of fission and fusion
feedback: Energy coming from the Sun is due to the fusion of hydrogen nuclei into helium nuclei, which releases huge amounts of energy. An extremely high temperature and pressure at the centre of the Sun creates the conditions for nuclear fusion.

subject: Physics
q: During the fission of \[1\] kg of uranium \[-235\], _____ energy is released.
skill: Describe the processes and practical applications of fission and fusion
feedback:
In each fission reaction, a large amount of energy is released.
During the fission of \[1\] kg of uranium \[-235\] about \[67\] \[\times\] \[10^{10}\] J of energy is released.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following major historical disasters involved long term radiation hazards on a massive scale?
I.   the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
II.  the Chernobyl accident
III. the September 11 attacks
skill: Describe the hazards of radioactive materials
feedback:
With the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, the world saw its first ever deployment of a nuclear fission bomb. Hiroshima and Nagasaki are two cities of Japan on which atomic bombs were dropped by American B-29 bombers, on August 6 and August 9, 1945, during World War II. Each bombing instantly killed tens of thousands of people; approximately \[200,000\] collectively. The effects of radiation remained within the cities with different types of cancers, leukaemia, miscarriages, birth defects, and mental and physical disabilities kept occurring until about ten years after the explosions.

Chernobyl, near the city of Pripyat in Ukraine, was one of the largest and oldest nuclear power plants in the world, until April 26, 1986 when a mishandled experiment caused a series of nuclear explosions, killing thousands of people in the subsequent years from radiation poisoning and thyroid cancer. The radiation released in the Chernobyl accident was far more than that released during the Hiroshima-Nagasaki event, severely contaminating a large piece of land, farmland, air, and groundwater. Exposure to radiation gave birth to many health issues including leukaemia, thyroid cancer, and Down's syndrome. Enough land near the site is believed to not be habitable for as much as 150 years.

The September 11 attacks were plane-crash suicide attacks that took place in the US in 2001, killing about \[3,000\] people.

subject: Physics
q:
Radiation burns on the skin are usually caused by _____ radiations.
I.   alpha
II.  beta
III. gamma
skill: Describe the hazards of radioactive materials
feedback:
Radiation can cause pigmentation (darkening of certain patches) on the skin, or burns, like redness and soreness. This is usually caused by beta particles and gamma rays.
Alpha particles are the least penetrative of these radiations as they are large and heavy. They're usually blocked by clothing. If they do reach the skin, they still can't penetrate deep into it.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT a safety precaution regarding radiation and radioactive materials?
options: storing radioactive sources in open containers
handling radioactive sources with tongs and forceps
washing hands or bathing after possible exposure
skill: Describe the hazards of radioactive materials
feedback:
The following are some safety precautions to adhere to when dealing with radiation or radioactive material:
\[\bullet \] Avoid frequent visits to radiation-sensitive areas.
\[\bullet \] Store radioactive sources in lead containers, since the most penetrative and harmful of all radiations, gamma \[\gamma\] rays, can be blocked by lead.
\[\bullet \] Wear protective gear: lead clothing, rubber gloves, etc. while in radiation-sensitive areas and while handling radioactive materials.
\[\bullet \] Handle radioactive materials with tongs and forceps in order to avoid contamination through direct contact.
\[\bullet \] Wash hands and the body after every instance of exposure to radioactive radiations.

Which of the following are health hazards of radiation?
I.   cancers
II.  cataracts
III. thyroid diseases

feedback:
Radiation has many health hazards, including but not limited to:
\[\bullet \] cataracts or blurred vision
\[\bullet \] different types of cancer
\[\bullet \] genetic mutations causing abnormalities
\[\bullet \] sterility or the inability to reproduce
\[\bullet \] thyroid diseases
\[\bullet \] leukaemia, or blood cancer

subject: Physics
q: Which among the following radiations has more penetrating power\[?\]
options: All have the same penetrating ability.
skill: Describe the hazards of radioactive materials
feedback: Gamma rays have more penetrating power than beta and alpha radiations. Gamma radiations are high-energy light photons of very high frequency emitted by unstable excited nuclei.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following type(s) of radiation does not cause a nuclear transmutation?

I.   alpha (\[\alpha\]) particles
II.  beta (\[\beta\]) particles
III. gamma (\[\gamma\]) radiation
skill: Explain nuclear transmutations and the meaning of half-life of a radioactive material
feedback:
Nuclear transmutation is the process in which one chemical element is converted into another, along with the emission of certain radiations.
When this happens due to radioactive decay, a parent unstable nuclide changes to a stable daughter nuclide. Radioactive decay is the process in which an unstable nucleus emits any type of radioactive radiation. These radiations usually include \[\alpha\] particles, \[\beta\] particles, and \[\gamma\] radiation. Alpha and beta radiations are basically charged particles (helium atoms and electrons, respectively), and hence, their emission causes a nucleus to convert into a new nucleus \[-\] or transmute. Gamma radiations are not particles, and so they do not cause a transmutation.

subject: Physics
q: How many half-lives would it take for a sample of a radioactive element to reduce to \[12.5\%\] of its initial amount?
skill: Explain nuclear transmutations and the meaning of half-life of a radioactive material
feedback:
A half-life is the time taken for half of the unstable nuclei in a sample of a radioactive element to disintegrate. This means that after \[1\] half-life, \[50 \%\] of the original amount is left.

After \[2\] half-lives, \[\frac{50}{2}=25 \%\] of the original amount is left.

After \[\bf3\] half-lives, \[\frac{25}{2}=12.5 \%\] of the original amount is left.

subject: Physics
q: Nuclear transmutations are _____.
skill: Explain nuclear transmutations and the meaning of half-life of a radioactive material
feedback:
Nuclear transmutation is the changing of an element with unstable nuclei into an element with stable nuclei. For example, radium-\[226\] is an element with unstable nuclei. It undergoes nuclear transmutation by emitting alpha \[\alpha\] particles and turning into radon, which has stable nuclei.

These transmutations occur spontaneously, which means they occur without anything prompting the transmutations.
They occur randomly. This means that they can occur at any time without us being able to predict where or when they would occur.
These transmutations are radioactive. This means that they emit radioactive particles when they occur.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is natural radioactivity?
options: emissions of infrared radiation from hot glowing metal
emissions of microwave radiation in microwave ovens
emissions from unstable nuclei to make them stable
radiated signals from cell phone towers
skill: Explain nuclear transmutations and the meaning of half-life of a radioactive material
feedback:
Natural radioactivity occurs when unstable nuclei of atoms emit radiation to become stable elements. In this process, nuclear transmutations occur.

Nuclear transmutation is the changing of an element with unstable nuclei into an element with stable nuclei. For example, radium-\[226\] is an element with unstable nuclei. It undergoes nuclear transmutation by emitting alpha \[\alpha\] particles and turning into radon, which has stable nuclei.
